tips: Tip: Counter-canter should feel like normal canter with a slight lean. If it feels like a struggle, the horse needs more basic canter balance first.

title: Counter-Canter Shallow Loop
steps: Place a cone 5 meters from the rail at the midpoint of the long side.
Establish true canter on the right lead, tracking right.
At the first letter of the long side, begin a gentle loop off the track toward the cone.
Pass the 5-meter cone in counter-canter (you're on the right lead but curving left).
Return to the track at the last letter of the long side, back in true canter.
The loop should be smooth and gradual, not a sharp V-shape.
Repeat 3-4 times, then change leads and practice on the other rein.
mistakes: The horse swapping leads at the deepest point of the loop.
The loop becoming too shallow (only 2-3 meters) because the rider doesn't commit.
Losing the three-beat canter rhythm during the counter-canter portion.
The horse becoming tense and tight in the back through the loop.
variations: Deepen the loop to 10 meters for a more demanding counter-canter exercise.
Chain shallow loops on both long sides without changing leads.
Add a flying change at the end of the loop back to the track.
tips: Tip: Maintain a slight flexion toward the lead throughout the loop. The horse's body stays almost straight; only the flexion tells it which lead to maintain.

title: Flying Change Preparation: Late Lead Change
steps: This exercise prepares the aids for flying changes without demanding a clean change.
Establish a left-lead canter on a 20-meter circle at B.
Ride across the diagonal toward E, maintaining the left lead (counter-canter).
At or just before E, ask for a downward transition to trot for 1-2 strides, then pick up the right lead.
The goal is to reduce the trot strides until the horse eventually changes leads without trot.
Start with 3-4 trot strides and gradually reduce to 1-2 over multiple sessions.
Don't force the flying change. Let it develop naturally as the horse becomes more balanced and responsive.
mistakes: Rushing the exercise and asking for a flying change before the horse is ready.
The horse becoming anxious and running because it senses the rider's urgency.
Not enough preparation in counter-canter, so the horse swaps leads prematurely.
Using a strong, dramatic aid that scares the horse instead of a clear, calm request.
variations: Practice the late change on a figure-eight at X.
Use a ground pole at the change point to help the horse jump into the new lead.
Combine with counter-canter work to build the horse's confidence in holding a lead.
tips: Tip: Flying changes come from preparation, not force. A horse that can do clean simple changes with 1-2 walk/trot strides is ready to attempt flying changes.

title: Flying Change on the Diagonal
steps: Establish a balanced canter on the left lead. Collect for 3-4 strides to prepare.
Turn across the diagonal. Maintain the left lead until you pass X.
After X, straighten the horse completely for 1-2 strides.
Apply the new outside leg (left leg) behind the girth while shifting your weight slightly to the new inside seat bone.
The horse should change both front and hind legs in a single stride. This is a clean flying change.
If the horse only changes in front (late behind), return to trot and try again later.
Practice 2-3 changes per session maximum. Quality over quantity.
mistakes: Asking for the change too early before the horse is straight.
Late behind — the horse changes the front legs but not the hind legs in the same stride.
The horse becoming tense and running after the change.
The rider throwing their body or swinging their legs dramatically to ask for the change.
variations: Practice changes on the center line at specific letters for precision.
Work toward tempi changes (every 4 strides, every 3, every 2, every stride).
Ride a figure-eight with flying changes at X.
tips: Tip: A good flying change looks effortless. If it looks dramatic, something in the preparation was missing. Go back to simple changes and try again.

title: Canter Pirouette Preparation
steps: Begin at the collected canter on a 20-meter circle. The canter should be very collected with active hind legs.
Over 2-3 revolutions, decrease the circle size: 20 to 15 to 10 meters.
On the 10-meter circle, the horse should be nearly performing a canter pirouette — the inside hind leg steps in a very small circle.
Maintain the canter rhythm. The hind legs must keep the three-beat pattern.
Hold the smallest circle for 2-3 strides, then spiral back out to 20 meters.
The entire exercise should feel like a coiled spring: the smaller the circle, the more engaged the haunches.
Walk and stretch. Pirouette work is extremely demanding on the horse's hind legs.
mistakes: The canter becoming four-beat as the circle shrinks (loss of collection).
The horse falling onto the inside shoulder instead of sitting on the inside hind.
Losing the canter entirely and breaking to trot on the small circle.
The rider leaning inward, throwing the horse off balance.
variations: Practice walk pirouettes first to establish the concept of turning on the haunches.
Work the canter spiral only down to 12-15 meters until the horse builds strength.
Add a flying change after spiraling out for a combination exercise.
tips: Tip: Pirouette work is like strength training. Start with what your horse can do well and gradually ask for more. Never force a smaller circle than the horse can maintain quality.

title: Metronome Trot Exercise
steps: Choose a working trot tempo. Count the beats in your head: 'one-two, one-two' for trot.
Ride the entire arena perimeter at this exact tempo. Use the long sides to check consistency.
If you have a metronome app, set it to your trot tempo and ride to the beat.
Notice where the tempo changes: typically in corners (slower) and on long sides (faster).
Adjust your aids to maintain the same tempo everywhere: use more leg in corners, half-halt on long sides.
Ride for 5 minutes at the exact same tempo, then walk and rest.
mistakes: Speeding up on the long side because the horse has more room.
Slowing down in corners because the bend disrupts the rhythm.
Confusing rhythm (the beat) with speed (how fast the horse travels).
Changing posting speed, which changes the horse's tempo.
variations: Perform the metronome exercise at the canter for three-beat rhythm work.
Add transitions while maintaining the same underlying tempo intention.
Practice at different tempos: a slower working trot vs. a more forward working trot.
tips: Tip: Rhythm is the first pillar of the training scale for a reason. Without consistent rhythm, nothing else works.

title: Posting Trot Diagonal Awareness
steps: At the posting trot, notice which diagonal you're rising on. You should rise when the outside front leg comes forward.
Track right and check: are you rising when the left front leg goes forward? If so, you're on the correct diagonal.
Change direction. Sit one extra beat to switch your posting diagonal, then continue on the new correct diagonal.
Practice checking and changing your diagonal 6-8 times, changing direction each time.
Progress to checking your diagonal without looking: feel which hind leg is pushing you up.
Make diagonal awareness automatic — check it every time you change direction.
mistakes: Rising on the wrong diagonal and not noticing.
Bouncing in the saddle when trying to change the diagonal (sit-sit instead of a smooth extra sit).
Only being able to check the diagonal by looking down at the shoulders.
Not changing the diagonal when changing direction.
variations: Practice riding on the wrong diagonal intentionally for 10 strides to feel the difference.
Change diagonals every 8 strides without changing direction for body awareness.
Close your eyes for 3-4 strides and feel which diagonal you're on.
tips: Tip: The posting diagonal matters because it determines which hind leg gets the most support. Correct diagonal aids collection on circles.

title: Half-Halt Timing Exercise
steps: At the working trot, plan half-halts at every arena letter around the perimeter.
A half-halt is a brief, simultaneous action: close your fingers on the reins, engage your core, and lighten your seat.
The half-halt lasts less than one stride. Immediately soften again after the half-halt.
At each letter, execute one crisp half-halt. The horse should briefly lighten the forehand and step more under with the hind legs.
After circling the arena once with half-halts at every letter, evaluate: did each half-halt produce a response?
If the horse ignored a half-halt, make the next one slightly stronger. If it over-reacted, make it softer.
mistakes: The half-halt being too long, becoming a full halt or a significant slow-down.
Using only the reins without the seat and core, creating a pulling half-halt.
Not releasing after the half-halt, so the horse never gets the reward of softened aids.
Every half-halt being the same intensity regardless of the horse's response.
variations: Add half-halts before every transition for seamless gait changes.
Practice half-halts at the canter for three-beat timing.
Use half-halts to adjust the stride length without changing the gait.
tips: Tip: A half-halt is like tapping someone on the shoulder — brief, clear, and followed by release. It's a conversation, not a command.

title: Counting Strides Between Letters
steps: At the working trot, count the number of strides from one letter to the next along the long side.
Ride the long side and count: H to E might be 12 strides, for example.
Now ride the same long side and try to fit exactly 12 strides again. Same rhythm, same stride length.
Change it: try to fit 14 strides (shorter, more collected) or 10 strides (longer, more extended).
Ride back and forth, changing the stride count target each time.
This exercise teaches you to feel and control stride length, which is essential for jumping and dressage.
mistakes: Losing count because the rider stops focusing mid-way.
Changing the tempo instead of the stride length to hit the count.
Only being able to lengthen but not shorten (or vice versa).
Counting every stride of the posting trot instead of every other one (posting trot has two phases per stride).
variations: Count strides on a diagonal and adjust: lengthen or shorten to hit a target number.
Apply the counting exercise at the canter for jumping preparation.
Count strides in a combination: letter A to B = 12, letter B to C = 14 (different stride lengths).
tips: Tip: This skill directly transfers to jumping distances. If you can adjust flatwork strides on demand, you can ride any distance to a fence.

title: Intro Test A Walk-Trot Pattern
steps: This simulates a basic Intro Level dressage test pattern. Enter at A at the working trot.
Halt at X. Salute (nod). Proceed at the working trot.
Track right at C. Ride the long side, then across the diagonal H-X-F.
At F, continue tracking left. Ride a 20-meter circle at A.
Continue on the track. Free walk across the next diagonal, then pick up trot before the corner.
Ride the remaining pattern, finishing with a halt at X. Salute.
Practice the complete pattern 2-3 times, focusing on accuracy and smooth transitions.
mistakes: Not halting at X precisely — stopping too early or too late on the center line.
The diagonal line drifting instead of being straight from letter to letter.
Forgetting the pattern mid-test and losing your place.
Not preparing transitions early enough, causing them to happen at the wrong spot.
variations: Practice individual movements from the test in isolation before stringing them together.
Have someone call the test for you while you ride.
Ride the test at the walk first to memorize the pattern without worrying about trot balance.
tips: Tip: Memorize the test by drawing it on paper, then walking it on foot in the arena. Ride it only after you can walk it perfectly.

title: Training Level Center Line and Halt
steps: Turn down the center line at A at the working trot.
Focus your eyes on C. Ride a perfectly straight line.
At X, prepare to halt. Half-halt 3 strides before X.
Halt at X. The halt should be square with the horse standing straight on the center line.
Stand immobile for 3 seconds. Then proceed at the working trot toward C.
At C, turn left or right onto the track. The turn should be smooth and balanced.
Repeat the center line entry and halt 4-6 times, alternating the direction at C.
mistakes: Drifting off the center line because the horse is drawn to the rail or other horses.
The halt not being square — one leg trailing or the horse standing crooked.
Moving at the halt (fidgeting, stepping, swishing tail).
The transition from halt to trot being sluggish or explosive.
variations: Add a rein-back after the halt for Training Level Test 3 preparation.
Practice the entry at medium trot for Second Level test preparation.
Film the entry from C to assess your straightness objectively.
tips: Tip: First impressions matter in dressage tests. Your center line entry is the judge's first view of your horse. Make it count.

title: Free Walk to Medium Walk Transition
steps: At the free walk, allow maximum rein length. The horse should stretch its neck forward and down with long, ground-covering strides.
5-6 strides before your transition point, begin slowly taking up the reins.
Shorten the reins gradually, not abruptly. The horse should accept the contact without raising its head sharply.
Transition to medium walk: the horse shortens its frame but maintains energy and rhythm.
The medium walk should feel purposeful and marching, not restricted or sluggish.
Practice this transition 4-6 times. It appears in every dressage test and is often scored poorly.
mistakes: Snatching up the reins abruptly, causing the horse to hollow its back.
The horse losing energy in the transition, going from a big free walk to a shuffling medium walk.
Not allowing enough strides for the transition — it should take 3-4 strides to smoothly take up the contact.
The horse raising its head and bracing against the new contact.
variations: Practice the reverse: medium walk to free walk, which is equally important in tests.
Add a trot transition immediately after establishing medium walk.
Use the free walk to medium walk as a test for throughness — a tense horse won't transition smoothly.
tips: Tip: This transition is one of the most frequently penalized movements in dressage. Practice it until the rein-taking feels invisible.

title: Trot-Halt-Trot on the Center Line
steps: Enter the center line at A at the working trot.
Halt at D (the first quarter-line marker area). Stand for 2-3 seconds.
Proceed at the trot to X. Halt again. Stand for 2-3 seconds.
Proceed at the trot to G. Halt a final time. Stand for 2-3 seconds.
Proceed at the trot to C. Turn onto the track.
Each halt must be square and on the center line. Each trot departure must be prompt and balanced.
mistakes: Drifting off the center line between halts.
The halts not being square because the horse anticipates moving again.
Over-preparing the halt so the horse stops early.
The trot departures being sluggish from repeated halts.
variations: Add rein-back between each halt for a more advanced version.
Perform at the canter with halt transitions.
Use the exercise as a warm-up for test preparation.
tips: Tip: Multiple halts on the center line test your horse's patience and your precision. Each halt should feel like a deliberate choice, not an accident.

title: Working Trot to Free Walk and Back
steps: At the working trot, ride the long side. As you approach the diagonal, transition to walk.
Immediately allow the reins to lengthen for a free walk across the diagonal.
The horse should stretch its neck forward and down, taking big, relaxed strides.
Before reaching the opposite track, gradually shorten the reins to medium walk.
Pick up the working trot before the corner.
The entire sequence: trot → walk → free walk → medium walk → trot should flow seamlessly.
mistakes: The horse jogging in the walk transition instead of truly walking.
Not allowing enough rein for a genuine free walk.
Rushing the transition back to trot instead of re-establishing the walk contact first.
The horse not stretching down in the free walk, indicating tension in the topline.
variations: Perform the free walk on a curved line (20-meter circle) instead of the diagonal.
Add a halt on the diagonal before the free walk to test obedience.
Time the free walk: 10+ seconds of genuine relaxation before gathering up.
tips: Tip: The free walk is a window into your training. A horse that stretches freely forward-down is working correctly through its topline.

title: Turn Down the Center Line at Canter
steps: Establish a balanced collected canter on the track.
At the end of the long side, ride deep into the corner to set up your turn.
Turn down the center line at A, maintaining the canter.
The center line at canter reveals every straightness issue — the horse will drift toward the rail or lean.
Keep equal pressure in both legs and both reins. Look at C and ride toward it.
At C, turn onto the track on the lead you're already cantering.
Repeat on both leads 3-4 times. Note which lead makes the center line harder.
mistakes: The horse falling to one side consistently, revealing its natural crookedness.
Over-correcting with the reins, creating a zigzag instead of a straight line.
Losing the canter quality while focusing on straightness.
Not riding deep enough into the corner, making the turn too sharp.
variations: Add a halt at X from the canter for test preparation.
Ride a flying change on the center line at X.
Perform medium canter on the center line for Second Level preparation.
tips: Tip: The center line at canter is the ultimate straightness test. If your horse drifts left on the right lead, you know the right shoulder is dominant.

title: Trot Lengthening on a Circle
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle. Establish a balanced working trot.
On the open half of the circle (away from the wall), ask the horse to lengthen its stride.
The lengthening should last for about half the circle (from one quarter-point to the opposite one).
Collect back to working trot for the wall-side half of the circle.
Alternate lengthening and collecting every half-circle for 3-4 complete revolutions.
The challenge is maintaining the circle shape while changing the stride length.
mistakes: The circle growing larger during the lengthening because the horse drifts outward.
Losing the rhythm — the trot should stay rhythmic even as the stride changes.
The horse falling onto the forehand during the lengthening portion.
Not collecting enough on the short side of the circle, so the lengthening contrast is lost.
variations: Try the exercise on a 15-meter circle for increased difficulty.
Alternate between trot lengthening and canter transitions on the circle.
Add lengthening on both halves of the circle but with collection for 2-3 strides at each quarter point.
tips: Tip: Lengthening on a circle is harder than on a straight line because the horse must maintain bend while extending. It's excellent for developing true throughness.

title: Canter to Walk Transition
steps: Establish a very collected canter on a 20-meter circle.
At your chosen marker, close your seat and both legs while closing your fingers on the reins.
The horse should transition directly from canter to walk — no trot strides in between.
The walk should be clear and forward, not a shuffle or a stagger.
Walk for 5-6 strides, then pick up canter again.
This is one of the most demanding downward transitions. It requires genuine collection.
mistakes: Trot strides creeping in between the canter and the walk.
The horse falling on the forehand in the transition, creating a stumbling walk.
Using too much rein and not enough seat, pulling the horse into the walk.
The walk being tense and tight after the transition instead of forward and swinging.
variations: Perform canter-walk on the long side at specific letters.
Chain canter-walk-canter for simple change practice.
Combine with rein-back: canter-walk-rein back-canter.
tips: Tip: The canter-to-walk transition is a litmus test for collection. If you can't do it cleanly, your canter isn't collected enough yet.

title: Lengthening and Shortening at Canter
steps: At the working canter, ride the long side. Divide it into thirds using letters as markers.
In the first third, collect the canter: shorter, more uphill strides.
In the second third, lengthen: bigger, more ground-covering strides without speeding up.
In the third third, collect again to prepare for the corner.
The transitions between collected and lengthened should be gradual, not sudden.
Repeat on both long sides and in both directions.
mistakes: Speeding up instead of lengthening — the stride should get bigger, not faster.
The transitions being too abrupt, like hitting a gas pedal and brake.
Not enough difference between the collected and lengthened canter.
Losing straightness during the adjustability changes.
variations: Add a medium canter section between the collected and lengthened for three levels of adjustability.
Perform the exercise across the diagonal instead of the long side.
Count strides in each section to ensure you're hitting consistent targets.
tips: Tip: Adjustability is the difference between a well-trained horse and a green one. The more range you have between shortest and longest stride, the more versatile your horse.

title: Leg Yield at the Canter
steps: Establish a balanced canter on the right lead. Turn onto the quarter line from the short side.
From the quarter line, apply your right leg to push the horse sideways toward the rail in a canter leg yield.
Maintain a slight left flexion (away from the direction of sideways travel).
The horse should move both forward and sideways, reaching the rail by the end of the long side.
The canter should remain three-beat and rhythmic throughout the lateral movement.
Walk and change direction. Repeat from the other quarter line on the new lead.
mistakes: The canter breaking to trot because the lateral movement disrupts the balance.
Too much sideways and not enough forward, causing the horse to lose impulsion.
The horse swapping leads during the lateral movement.
The rider leaning to one side, throwing the horse off balance.
variations: Start with just 3-4 sideways strides at canter before straightening.
Progress to canter leg yield across the full diagonal.
Use canter leg yield as preparation for canter half-pass.
tips: Tip: Canter leg yield is a stepping stone to half-pass. If your horse can stay balanced moving sideways at canter, it's ready for more advanced lateral work.

title: Dressage Pattern: Full Arena Workout
steps: This combines multiple movements into a flowing workout. Start at A, trot down the center line.
At C, track right. Ride a 20-meter circle at B. Continue on the track.
Across the diagonal H-X-F with medium trot. Collect before F.
At A, ride a 20-meter circle left. Continue on the track.
At B, shoulder-in down the long side to the corner. Ride the short side normally.
Across the diagonal K-X-M with free walk. Pick up trot before M.
At C, canter right lead. 20-meter circle at E. Continue to the next corner, then trot.
mistakes: Forgetting the sequence mid-workout.
Not preparing transitions soon enough, causing rushed or late changes.
Losing the quality of individual movements while focusing on the overall pattern.
Rushing through the workout instead of riding each movement deliberately.
variations: Create your own workout by combining movements you need to practice.
Add more canter work for a conditioning-focused session.
Include lateral movements on every long side for a suppleness-focused workout.
tips: Tip: Riding a connected sequence is harder than practicing movements in isolation. It's the closest thing to riding a dressage test.

title: Counter-Canter Serpentine
steps: Establish a balanced right-lead canter on the track.
Ride a shallow serpentine along the long side, maintaining the right lead throughout.
The loop should take you 5 meters off the track and back. You will be in counter-canter on the inward portion.
Maintain a slight right flexion even when curving left. The horse must hold the lead from your aids, not the direction.
Ride 3-4 shallow loops along the long side, then straighten and walk.
Change leads and repeat the counter-canter serpentine on the left lead.
mistakes: The horse swapping leads during the counter-canter portions.
The loops becoming too deep, making the counter-canter unstable.
Losing the three-beat rhythm in the counter-canter sections.
The rider gripping and becoming tense, transferring anxiety to the horse.
variations: Deepen the loops to 10 meters off the track for more demanding counter-canter.
Add flying changes at the peak of each loop for upper-level work.
Ride the serpentine in true canter, then immediately repeat in counter-canter for comparison.
tips: Tip: Counter-canter serpentines build the straightness and balance needed for flying changes. The horse learns to hold the lead until you say otherwise.

title: Half-Pass Zigzag at Trot
steps: Start on the center line at the collected trot. Half-pass to the right, moving diagonally toward the right track.
After reaching the quarter line, straighten for 2-3 strides, then change the bend and half-pass to the left.
Continue the zigzag: half-pass right to the quarter line, straighten, half-pass left to the opposite quarter line.
Each half-pass segment should cover equal distance both forward and sideways.
The changes of bend should happen in the 2-3 straight strides. The horse must be supple enough to switch quickly.
Complete 4 direction changes (2 zigzags), then ride forward on the center line and walk.
mistakes: The segments being uneven — more sideways in one direction than the other.
The horse losing collection during the direction changes.
Not enough straight strides between half-pass directions, causing a choppy transition.
The haunches leading instead of the shoulders in the half-pass portions.
variations: Progress to half-pass zigzag at the collected canter.
Increase to 6 direction changes for a longer exercise.
Vary the width of each zigzag segment for adjustability training.
tips: Tip: The half-pass zigzag is one of the most demanding dressage movements. Master half-pass in isolation before attempting zigzags.

title: Piaffe Introduction: Walk-Trot-Walk
steps: Piaffe is the ultimate collected trot: the horse trots in place. This introduction builds the concept gradually.
At the collected walk, use both legs rhythmically to ask the horse for more energy without more forward speed.
Touch the horse behind your leg with a dressage whip in a rhythmic tap to encourage more activity.
The horse should begin taking shorter, higher steps — somewhere between walk and trot.
Don't ask for a full piaffe yet. Accept any increase in energy and step height as progress.
Release the aids and walk forward on a long rein. Repeat 3-4 times maximum per session.
mistakes: Asking for too much too soon — piaffe takes months or years to develop.
The horse running forward instead of collecting the energy upward.
Over-use of the whip creating tension and anxiety instead of engagement.
Not rewarding small improvements, frustrating the horse.
variations: Work with a trainer on the ground who can assist with the whip timing.
Practice in-hand piaffe before attempting under saddle.
Combine with transitions: collected walk to piaffe steps to collected trot forward.
tips: Tip: Piaffe is not something you force. It emerges from progressive collection work over many months. Celebrate each small step of progress.

title: Extended Walk Development
steps: Start at a medium walk with a steady contact. The horse should be marching purposefully.
Gradually lengthen the reins, encouraging the horse to stretch its neck forward and down.
As the horse stretches, the walk strides should naturally lengthen. Allow this to happen.
The extended walk should cover maximum ground with each stride. The hind feet should overstep the front feet prints.
Maintain the four-beat walk rhythm. The extension should be in stride length, not speed.
Walk for 2-3 minutes in the extended walk, then gradually collect back to medium walk.
mistakes: Confusing extended walk with a fast walk — the tempo should stay the same.
Not allowing enough rein for the horse to truly stretch and extend.
The walk losing its four-beat rhythm and becoming lateral (pacing).
Pulling the reins back too quickly when collecting from extended to medium.
variations: Practice extended walk on the diagonal across the arena.
Compare the horse's overstep in medium walk vs. extended walk to measure improvement.
Use extended walk as a reward between demanding exercises.
tips: Tip: The extended walk is one of the highest-scored movements in dressage because it reveals the horse's natural scope. You can't fake a good walk.

title: Transition Quickfire Drill
steps: This drill tests rapid-fire transitions to sharpen both horse and rider.
At A: walk. At F: trot. At B: walk. At M: trot. At C: halt. At H: walk. At E: canter. At K: trot. At A: halt.
Each transition must happen at the letter — not before, not after.
The rapid changes require precise planning. Prepare each transition 3-4 strides ahead.
Ride the full sequence 2-3 times. Each pass should improve in accuracy and smoothness.
The horse will start to anticipate — vary the pattern to keep it guessing.
mistakes: Transitions happening too late because the rider didn't prepare early enough.
The horse anticipating and changing gaits before the letter.
Losing track of which transition comes at which letter.
Becoming frustrated and riding roughly instead of clearly.
variations: Create your own transition sequences for variety.
Add canter-walk transitions for advanced responsiveness.
Have someone call the transitions randomly for unpredictability.
tips: Tip: This drill is like musical chairs for riders. The faster and more accurate your transitions, the sharper your aids become.

title: Flying Change Preparation: Late Lead Change at the Walk
steps: This exercise prepares the aids for flying changes without demanding a clean change.
Establish a left-lead walk on a 20-meter circle at B.
Ride across the diagonal toward E, maintaining the left lead (counter-walk).
At or just before E, ask for a downward transition to walk for 1-2 strides, then pick up the right lead.
The goal is to reduce the walk strides until the horse eventually changes leads without walk.
Start with 3-4 walk strides and gradually reduce to 1-2 over multiple sessions.
Don't force the flying change. Let it develop naturally as the horse becomes more balanced and responsive.
mistakes: Rushing the exercise and asking for a flying change before the horse is ready.
The horse becoming anxious and running because it senses the rider's urgency.
Not enough preparation in counter-canter, so the horse swaps leads prematurely.
Using a strong, dramatic aid that scares the horse instead of a clear, calm request.
variations: Practice the late change on a figure-eight at X.
Use a ground pole at the change point to help the horse jump into the new lead.
Combine with counter-canter work to build the horse's confidence in holding a lead.
tips: Tip: Flying changes come from preparation, not force. A horse that can do clean simple changes with 1-2 walk/trot strides is ready to attempt flying changes.

The goal is to reduce the trot strides until the horse eventually changes leads without trot.

Don't force the flying change. Let it develop naturally as the horse becomes more balanced and responsive.

The goal is to reduce the collected trot strides until the horse eventually changes leads without collected trot.

Don't force the flying change. Let it develop naturally as the horse becomes more balanced and responsive.

variations: Practice the late change on a figure-eight at X.

tips: Tip: Flying changes come from preparation, not force. A horse that can do clean simple changes with 1-2 walk/trot strides is ready to attempt flying changes.

title: Yielding the Inside Rein on a Circle at the Walk
steps: Establish a balanced working walk on a 20-meter circle with correct bend.
When the circle feels steady and balanced, briefly give away the inside rein by pushing your hand forward 4-6 inches.
The horse should maintain the circle, the bend, and the tempo without the inside rein for 3-5 strides.
Take back the inside rein and re-establish your normal contact.
If the horse falls in or loses bend when you release the inside rein, the horse is leaning on that rein rather than carrying itself.
Repeat the give-and-take 4-5 times per direction. The horse should improve with each attempt.
mistakes: Giving the rein too suddenly, startling the horse and causing a wobble.
The horse immediately falling to the inside, proving it was leaning on the inside rein.
Not giving enough rein — a tiny 1-inch give doesn't test anything.
Only testing on the horse's easy side and avoiding the difficult side.
variations: Try yielding both reins briefly for a true self-carriage test.
Perform the test at the canter for increased difficulty.
Give the inside rein on a 15-meter circle where balance is harder.
tips: Tip: This test tells you the truth: if your horse can't hold the circle without the inside rein, it's not truly balanced and through.

title: Transition at Every Letter at the Walk
steps: Using the arena letters as markers, plan a transition at every letter around the perimeter.
A to K: walk. K to E: walk. E to H: walk. H to C: walk. And so on.
The transitions must happen exactly at the letter — not before, not after.
This requires precise planning. Begin preparing 3-4 strides before each letter.
After mastering walk-walk, upgrade to walk-walk transitions at the letters.
Focus on the quality of each transition rather than the speed of moving between them.
mistakes: Transitions happening early because the rider rushes the preparation.
Transitions happening late because the horse doesn't respond promptly.
Losing count of which transition comes next and getting confused.
The horse anticipating and becoming tense as it approaches each letter.
variations: Randomly vary which transitions happen at which letters for unpredictability.
Add halt transitions at some letters for three-gait work.
Perform the exercise with eyes closed for 2-3 strides to test your feel for the horse's position.
tips: Tip: Plan your ride before you start. Know which transition happens at which letter. Riding with a mental map makes your training deliberate.

This requires precise planning. Begin preparing 3-4 strides before each letter.
After mastering trot-trot, upgrade to trot-trot transitions at the letters.
Focus on the quality of each transition rather than the speed of moving between them.
mistakes: Transitions happening early because the rider rushes the preparation.
Transitions happening late because the horse doesn't respond promptly.

title: Leg Yield from Center Line to Wall at the Walk
steps: Start at A on the center line at the working walk.
Ride straight for 3-4 strides to establish rhythm and straightness on the center line.
Apply your left leg at the girth to leg yield to the right, moving the horse sideways toward the right wall.
Keep a slight left flexion (away from the direction of sideways travel). The horse's body should be nearly straight.
Maintain the forward tempo. The horse should reach the rail by the time you arrive at B or E.
Walk, change direction, and repeat from the center line to the other wall.
mistakes: Starting the leg yield before establishing straightness on the center line.
Too much neck bend, causing the shoulder to lead excessively.
The horse not reaching the wall, indicating insufficient lateral response to the leg aid.
Losing forward impulsion so the horse shuffles sideways without marching forward.
variations: Vary where you start the leg yield: start at C for a longer lateral distance.
Add a circle at B or E before completing the leg yield to the wall.
Increase speed to medium trot while maintaining the leg yield angle.
tips: Tip: Start with just a few sideways steps and build up. Three good steps sideways are better than ten crooked ones.

Add a circle at B or E before completing the leg yield to the wall.
Increase speed to medium trot while maintaining the leg yield angle.
tips: Tip: Start with just a few sideways steps and build up. Three good steps sideways are better than ten crooked ones.

title: Leg Yield from Center Line to Wall at the Sitting Trot
steps: Start at A on the center line at the working sitting trot.
Ride straight for 3-4 strides to establish rhythm and straightness on the center line.
Apply your left leg at the girth to leg yield to the right, moving the horse sideways toward the right wall.
Keep a slight left flexion (away from the direction of sideways travel). The horse's body should be nearly straight.
Maintain the forward tempo. The horse should reach the rail by the time you arrive at B or E.
Sitting Sitting Trot, change direction, and repeat from the center line to the other wall.
mistakes: Starting the leg yield before establishing straightness on the center line.
Too much neck bend, causing the shoulder to lead excessively.
The horse not reaching the wall, indicating insufficient lateral response to the leg aid.
Losing forward impulsion so the horse shuffles sideways without marching forward.
variations: Vary where you start the leg yield: start at C for a longer lateral distance.
Add a circle at B or E before completing the leg yield to the wall.
Increase speed to medium trot while maintaining the leg yield angle.
tips: Tip: Start with just a few sideways steps and build up. Three good steps sideways are better than ten crooked ones.

title: Leg Yield from Center Line to Wall at the Collected Trot

Collected Collected Trot, change direction, and repeat from the center line to the other wall.
mistakes: Starting the leg yield before establishing straightness on the center line.
Too much neck bend, causing the shoulder to lead excessively.
The horse not reaching the wall, indicating insufficient lateral response to the leg aid.
Losing forward impulsion so the horse shuffles sideways without marching forward.
variations: Vary where you start the leg yield: start at C for a longer lateral distance.

tips: Tip: Start with just a few sideways steps and build up. Three good steps sideways are better than ten crooked ones.

title: Neck Yielding at the Walk at the Walk
steps: At the walk, ask your horse to flex to the inside by softly vibrating the inside rein.
When the horse gives to the pressure and flexes, immediately soften (give) the rein as a reward.
Ask for the flexion again. Each time the horse softens, reward by giving the rein.
Repeat on the other side. You're teaching the horse to yield to rein pressure, not pull against it.
After 5-6 successful yields on each side, test by asking while walkting.
The goal is a horse that softens its jaw and poll when you ask, without resistance.
mistakes: Pulling the rein backward instead of vibrating or squeezing.
Not releasing (giving) the rein when the horse yields, so the horse never learns the reward.
Asking for too much flexion — you only need the horse to soften at the poll.
Getting into a tug-of-war where both horse and rider are pulling.
variations: Add neck yielding during trot work as a regular softening exercise.
Combine neck yielding with leg yielding for a multitask suppleness exercise.
Use one-rein flexion followed by both-rein half-halt for connection work.
tips: Tip: Think of the rein aid as asking a question, not giving a command. Ask, wait for the answer, then say thank you by softening.

title: Posting Walk Diagonal Awareness
steps: At the posting walk, notice which diagonal you're rising on. You should rise when the outside front leg comes forward.
Track right and check: are you rising when the left front leg goes forward? If so, you're on the correct diagonal.
Change direction. Sit one extra beat to switch your posting diagonal, then continue on the new correct diagonal.
Practice checking and changing your diagonal 6-8 times, changing direction each time.
Progress to checking your diagonal without looking: feel which hind leg is pushing you up.
Make diagonal awareness automatic — check it every time you change direction.
mistakes: Rising on the wrong diagonal and not noticing.
Bouncing in the saddle when trying to change the diagonal (sit-sit instead of a smooth extra sit).
Only being able to check the diagonal by looking down at the shoulders.
Not changing the diagonal when changing direction.
variations: Practice riding on the wrong diagonal intentionally for 10 strides to feel the difference.
Change diagonals every 8 strides without changing direction for body awareness.
Close your eyes for 3-4 strides and feel which diagonal you're on.
tips: Tip: The posting diagonal matters because it determines which hind leg gets the most support. Correct diagonal aids collection on circles.

title: Counter-Walk Serpentine
steps: Establish a balanced right-lead walk on the track.
Ride a shallow serpentine along the long side, maintaining the right lead throughout.
The loop should take you 5 meters off the track and back. You will be in counter-walk on the inward portion.
Maintain a slight right flexion even when curving left. The horse must hold the lead from your aids, not the direction.
Ride 3-4 shallow loops along the long side, then straighten and walk.
Change leads and repeat the counter-walk serpentine on the left lead.
mistakes: The horse swapping leads during the counter-canter portions.
The loops becoming too deep, making the counter-canter unstable.
Losing the three-beat rhythm in the counter-canter sections.
The rider gripping and becoming tense, transferring anxiety to the horse.
variations: Deepen the loops to 10 meters off the track for more demanding counter-canter.
Add flying changes at the peak of each loop for upper-level work.
Ride the serpentine in true canter, then immediately repeat in counter-canter for comparison.
tips: Tip: Counter-canter serpentines build the straightness and balance needed for flying changes. The horse learns to hold the lead until you say otherwise.

title: Stretchy Circle at the Walk
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle. Pick up a working walk with a steady contact.
Gradually allow the reins to slide through your fingers, giving your horse 2-3 inches of rein at a time.
Your horse should follow the contact downward, stretching the neck forward and down while maintaining the walk.
The circle should remain the same size and shape. The stretch should not cause the horse to fall onto the forehand or rush.
Maintain the stretch for 2-3 complete circles, keeping your leg on to support the rhythm.
Slowly take the reins back to working length, asking your horse to come back to the contact.
Repeat the give-and-retake 3-4 times. The stretch should improve with each repetition.
mistakes: Throwing the reins away all at once instead of gradually lengthening.
The horse falling onto the forehand and speeding up when given rein.
The circle growing larger as the horse loses balance during the stretch.
The rider rounding their back and collapsing the core when lengthening the reins.
variations: Try the stretchy circle at the canter for a more advanced relaxation exercise.
Add a few strides of medium trot on the stretchy circle to test throughness.
Alternate between collected circle and stretchy circle every 2 revolutions.
tips: Tip: The stretchy circle tests whether your horse is truly through and connected. A hollow horse will lift the head instead of stretching down.

title: Flying Change on the Diagonal at the Walk
steps: Establish a balanced walk on the left lead. Collect for 3-4 strides to prepare.
Turn across the diagonal. Maintain the left lead until you pass X.
After X, straighten the horse completely for 1-2 strides.
Apply the new outside leg (left leg) behind the girth while shifting your weight slightly to the new inside seat bone.
The horse should change both front and hind legs in a single stride. This is a clean flying change.
If the horse only changes in front (late behind), return to walk and try again later.
Practice 2-3 changes per session maximum. Quality over quantity.
mistakes: Asking for the change too early before the horse is straight.
Late behind — the horse changes the front legs but not the hind legs in the same stride.
The horse becoming tense and running after the change.
The rider throwing their body or swinging their legs dramatically to ask for the change.
variations: Practice changes on the center line at specific letters for precision.
Work toward tempi changes (every 4 strides, every 3, every 2, every stride).
Ride a figure-eight with flying changes at X.
tips: Tip: A good flying change looks effortless. If it looks dramatic, something in the preparation was missing. Go back to simple changes and try again.

The rider throwing their body or swinging their legs dramatically to ask for the change.
variations: Practice changes on the center line at specific letters for precision.
Work toward tempi changes (every 4 strides, every 3, every 2, every stride).
Ride a figure-eight with flying changes at X.
tips: Tip: A good flying change looks effortless. If it looks dramatic, something in the preparation was missing. Go back to simple changes and try again.

title: Flying Change on the Diagonal at the Collected Trot
steps: Establish a balanced collected trot on the left lead. Collect for 3-4 strides to prepare.
Turn across the diagonal. Maintain the left lead until you pass X.
After X, straighten the horse completely for 1-2 strides.
Apply the new outside leg (left leg) behind the girth while shifting your weight slightly to the new inside seat bone.
The horse should change both front and hind legs in a single stride. This is a clean flying change.
If the horse only changes in front (late behind), return to collected trot and try again later.
Practice 2-3 changes per session maximum. Quality over quantity.
mistakes: Asking for the change too early before the horse is straight.
Late behind — the horse changes the front legs but not the hind legs in the same stride.
The horse becoming tense and running after the change.
The rider throwing their body or swinging their legs dramatically to ask for the change.
variations: Practice changes on the center line at specific letters for precision.
Work toward tempi changes (every 4 strides, every 3, every 2, every stride).
Ride a figure-eight with flying changes at X.
tips: Tip: A good flying change looks effortless. If it looks dramatic, something in the preparation was missing. Go back to simple changes and try again.

title: Leg Yield Zigzag at the Walk
steps: Place cones at the quarter line and three-quarter line on both long sides to mark your targets.
Starting on the center line at the working walk, leg yield to the right toward the three-quarter line.
When you reach the three-quarter line, change the flexion and leg yield back to the left, crossing the center line to the quarter line.
Continue zigzagging: quarter line to three-quarter line and back.
Each leg yield segment should cover equal distance both forward and sideways.
Focus on smooth transitions between directions. The change of flexion should happen in 2-3 straight strides.
Complete 4-6 zigzag segments, then walk and stretch.
mistakes: The segments becoming uneven — more sideways in one direction than the other.
Not allowing enough straight strides between direction changes.
The horse becoming tense or resistant from the constant directional changes.
Losing forward energy as the zigzag progresses.
variations: Start with just 2 direction changes (one zigzag) and build up to 6.
Ride the zigzag on the center line instead of the quarter lines for more room.
Progress to half-pass zigzag at the collected trot for upper-level work.
tips: Tip: Quality over quantity. Two clean direction changes are better than six sloppy ones.

title: Working Trot to Free Walk and Back at the Walk
steps: At the working walk, ride the long side. As you approach the diagonal, transition to walk.
Immediately allow the reins to lengthen for a free walk across the diagonal.
The horse should stretch its neck forward and down, taking big, relaxed strides.
Before reaching the opposite track, gradually shorten the reins to medium walk.
Pick up the working walk before the corner.
The entire sequence: walk → walk → free walk → medium walk → walk should flow seamlessly.
mistakes: The horse jogging in the walk transition instead of truly walking.
Not allowing enough rein for a genuine free walk.
Rushing the transition back to trot instead of re-establishing the walk contact first.
The horse not stretching down in the free walk, indicating tension in the topline.
variations: Perform the free walk on a curved line (20-meter circle) instead of the diagonal.
Add a halt on the diagonal before the free walk to test obedience.
Time the free walk: 10+ seconds of genuine relaxation before gathering up.
tips: Tip: The free walk is a window into your training. A horse that stretches freely forward-down is working correctly through its topline.

title: Turn Down the Center Line at Walk
steps: Establish a balanced collected walk on the track.
At the end of the long side, ride deep into the corner to set up your turn.
Turn down the center line at A, maintaining the walk.
The center line at walk reveals every straightness issue — the horse will drift toward the rail or lean.
Keep equal pressure in both legs and both reins. Look at C and ride toward it.
At C, turn onto the track on the lead you're already walking.
Repeat on both leads 3-4 times. Note which lead makes the center line harder.
mistakes: The horse falling to one side consistently, revealing its natural crookedness.
Over-correcting with the reins, creating a zigzag instead of a straight line.
Losing the canter quality while focusing on straightness.
Not riding deep enough into the corner, making the turn too sharp.
variations: Add a halt at X from the canter for test preparation.
Ride a flying change on the center line at X.
Perform medium canter on the center line for Second Level preparation.
tips: Tip: The center line at canter is the ultimate straightness test. If your horse drifts left on the right lead, you know the right shoulder is dominant.

title: Shoulder-In on the Rail at the Walk
steps: Begin at the working walk, tracking right along the long side of the arena.
As you come out of the corner, maintain the bend you had in the corner but continue straight down the long side.
Your horse should be on three tracks: the inside hind leg stepping toward the line of the outside front leg.
Keep your inside leg at the girth to maintain impulsion and bend. Your outside leg behind the girth prevents the haunches from swinging out.
Your inside rein maintains flexion while the outside rein controls the degree of angle. Aim for about 30 degrees from the rail.
Ride shoulder-in for the length of the long side, then ride straight into the corner.
Repeat on the other rein. Most horses find shoulder-in easier on one side than the other.
mistakes: Too much neck bend without enough body angle, creating neck-in instead of shoulder-in.
Losing impulsion because the rider over-uses the reins to create the angle.
The angle varying along the long side instead of remaining consistent.
The horse drifting off the rail, turning instead of moving laterally.
variations: Ride shoulder-in at the walk first to establish the feel before adding trot.
Progress to shoulder-in at the collected trot for increased engagement.
Use shoulder-in as a preparation for half-pass: shoulder-in to half-pass mid-arena.
tips: Tip: Think of riding the horse's shoulders around your inside leg like wrapping a scarf. The bend comes from the body, not just the neck.

title: Three-Loop Walk Serpentine with Simple Changes
steps: Set up a three-loop serpentine with cones marking the center line crossings.
Begin at the walk on the correct lead for the first loop.
As you cross the center line, transition to walk for 3-4 steps, then pick up the new lead for the second loop.
The simple change should happen exactly at the center line, not before or after.
Continue through all three loops, performing a simple change at each center line crossing.
Maintain the same loop size and shape throughout. Each loop should touch the long side.
Repeat the serpentine 2-3 times, then walk and stretch.
mistakes: The simple changes drifting away from the center line crossing point.
Loops becoming uneven because the rider focuses on the changes and forgets the geometry.
The horse becoming tense and anticipating the downward transitions.
Walk steps increasing as the exercise progresses because of fatigue.
variations: Use flying changes instead of simple changes for upper-level work.
Increase to four or five loops for a longer, more demanding exercise.
Add counter-canter through the loops instead of simple changes for balance training.
tips: Tip: Master the three-loop trot serpentine before attempting this. The canter version adds the complexity of lead changes to an already demanding pattern.

The simple change should happen exactly at the center line, not before or after.
Continue through all three loops, performing a simple change at each center line crossing.
Maintain the same loop size and shape throughout. Each loop should touch the long side.
Repeat the serpentine 2-3 times, then collected collected trot and stretch.
mistakes: The simple changes drifting away from the center line crossing point.
Loops becoming uneven because the rider focuses on the changes and forgets the geometry.
The horse becoming tense and anticipating the downward transitions.
Walk steps increasing as the exercise progresses because of fatigue.
variations: Use flying changes instead of simple changes for upper-level work.
Increase to four or five loops for a longer, more demanding exercise.
Add counter-canter through the loops instead of simple changes for balance training.
tips: Tip: Master the three-loop trot serpentine before attempting this. The canter version adds the complexity of lead changes to an already demanding pattern.

title: Diamond Pattern at the Walk
steps: Set up four cones at the midpoints of each arena side: A, B, C, and E (or equivalent points).
Starting at A, ride a straight line to B. Turn 90 degrees and ride straight to C.
Turn 90 degrees at C and ride straight to E. Turn again and ride straight back to A.
This creates a diamond shape in the arena. Each straight segment should be truly straight.
The 90-degree turns test your horse's response to your aids. Prepare each turn 3-4 strides in advance.
Ride the diamond at the walk first, then progress to the walk. At the walk, the turns become half-halts.
Reverse direction and ride the diamond the other way to work both reins equally.
mistakes: The straight segments curving instead of being laser-straight.
Over-shooting or under-shooting the 90-degree turns at each cone.
Losing impulsion during the turns because of over-use of the reins.
The horse anticipating turns and cutting corners before reaching the cone.
variations: Ride the diamond at the canter with simple changes at each turn point.
Add a halt at each cone, stand for 3 seconds, then proceed to the next point.
Make a smaller diamond using cones placed closer to the center for tighter turns.
tips: Tip: Prepare your turns like a car approaching a corner: slow the body first, then turn the steering.

title: Trot-Canter Transitions at the Trot
steps: Establish a balanced working trot on a 20-meter circle.
In a corner or at a circle marker, prepare for the trot transition: sit the trot for 2-3 strides.
Apply your outside leg behind the girth and your inside leg at the grit. Give a small half-halt, then allow the trot.
The first trot stride should be forward and balanced, not running or lurching.
Trot for half the circle, then transition back to trot. Sit the trot for 3-4 strides before rising.
Repeat the transition 4-6 times, then change direction and work the other lead.
mistakes: The horse picking up the wrong lead because the outside leg wasn't clear enough.
The transition being rough — the horse running into the canter instead of stepping up into it.
The rider throwing their body forward in the depart, unbalancing the horse.
Not sitting the trot before the transition, making the canter aid unclear.
variations: Practice trot-canter transitions on the long side at a specific letter.
Add walk-canter transitions for a more advanced departure.
Perform the canter depart from a 10-meter circle for improved engagement.
tips: Tip: The canter depart should feel like the horse lifts and opens the stride. If it feels like speeding up, you need more half-halt before the aid.

title: Free Walk to Medium Walk Transition at the Walk
steps: At the free walk, allow maximum rein length. The horse should stretch its neck forward and down with long, ground-covering strides.
5-6 strides before your transition point, begin slowly taking up the reins.
Shorten the reins gradually, not abruptly. The horse should accept the contact without raising its head sharply.
Transition to medium walk: the horse shortens its frame but maintains energy and rhythm.
The medium walk should feel purposeful and marching, not restricted or sluggish.
Practice this transition 4-6 times. It appears in every dressage test and is often scored poorly.
mistakes: Snatching up the reins abruptly, causing the horse to hollow its back.
The horse losing energy in the transition, going from a big free walk to a shuffling medium walk.
Not allowing enough strides for the transition — it should take 3-4 strides to smoothly take up the contact.
The horse raising its head and bracing against the new contact.
variations: Practice the reverse: medium walk to free walk, which is equally important in tests.
Add a trot transition immediately after establishing medium walk.
Use the free walk to medium walk as a test for throughness — a tense horse won't transition smoothly.
tips: Tip: This transition is one of the most frequently penalized movements in dressage. Practice it until the rein-taking feels invisible.

title: Extended Walk Development at the Walk
steps: Start at a medium walk with a steady contact. The horse should be marching purposefully.
Gradually lengthen the reins, encouraging the horse to stretch its neck forward and down.
As the horse stretches, the walk strides should naturally lengthen. Allow this to happen.
The extended walk should cover maximum ground with each stride. The hind feet should overstep the front feet prints.
Maintain the four-beat walk rhythm. The extension should be in stride length, not speed.
Walk for 2-3 minutes in the extended walk, then gradually collect back to medium walk.
mistakes: Confusing extended walk with a fast walk — the tempo should stay the same.
Not allowing enough rein for the horse to truly stretch and extend.
The walk losing its four-beat rhythm and becoming lateral (pacing).
Pulling the reins back too quickly when collecting from extended to medium.
variations: Practice extended walk on the diagonal across the arena.
Compare the horse's overstep in medium walk vs. extended walk to measure improvement.
Use extended walk as a reward between demanding exercises.
tips: Tip: The extended walk is one of the highest-scored movements in dressage because it reveals the horse's natural scope. You can't fake a good walk.

title: Tear-Drop Circle at the Walk
steps: A tear-drop shape combines a straight line with a half-circle. Start at A or C, ride straight toward X.
At X, begin a 10-meter half-circle turning either left or right.
Complete the half-circle so you are now facing back the way you came, aligned with the center line.
Ride straight back down the center line to your starting letter.
The key is the transition from straight to curved: your horse should accept the bend smoothly without resistance.
Repeat the tear-drop 3 times in each direction, improving the accuracy of your turns each time.
Progress by performing the tear-drop at the walk, maintaining rhythm through the shape change.
mistakes: Starting the half-circle too early or too late, missing the X centerpoint.
Losing straightness on the center line portions.
The horse bulging through the outside shoulder on the curved portion.
Rushing through the shape instead of maintaining a steady tempo.
variations: Ride the tear-drop at the canter, using a downward transition for the half-circle.
Make the half-circle portion smaller (8 meters) for more collection demand.
Chain two tear-drops together: one left, one right, creating a continuous flowing pattern.
tips: Tip: The tear-drop is excellent for test preparation since many dressage tests require riding to X and turning.

title: Shallow Loop on the Long Side at the Walk
steps: A shallow loop comes off the track 5 meters and returns. Place a cone 5 meters from the rail at the midpoint of the long side.
Track left along the long side. At the first quarter marker, begin a gentle curve inward toward the cone.
Pass the cone at the midpoint, 5 meters from the rail. The curve should be gradual, not a sharp turn.
After the cone, curve gently back to the rail, arriving at the third quarter marker of the long side.
The horse maintains the same bend throughout — you don't change the bend for this shallow loop.
Ride the shallow loop 3-4 times on each rein at the walk, focusing on a smooth, flowing arc.
mistakes: Coming too far off the track (more than 5 meters) or not far enough (less than 3 meters).
Changing the bend at the midpoint instead of maintaining the original bend.
The loop having a sharp angle at the departure and return points instead of smooth curves.
Looking at the cone instead of ahead to the return point on the rail.
variations: Increase the depth to 10 meters, requiring a change of bend at the midpoint.
Ride a shallow loop at the canter, which introduces counter-canter in the middle portion.
Chain shallow loops on both long sides for a continuous flowing exercise.
tips: Tip: Shallow loops are excellent for introducing counter-flexion and counter-canter in a gentle way.

title: Shallow Loop on the Long Side at the Trot
steps: A shallow loop comes off the track 5 meters and returns. Place a cone 5 meters from the rail at the midpoint of the long side.
Track left along the long side. At the first quarter marker, begin a gentle curve inward toward the cone.
Pass the cone at the midpoint, 5 meters from the rail. The curve should be gradual, not a sharp turn.
After the cone, curve gently back to the rail, arriving at the third quarter marker of the long side.
The horse maintains the same bend throughout — you don't change the bend for this shallow loop.
Ride the shallow loop 3-4 times on each rein at the trot, focusing on a smooth, flowing arc.
mistakes: Coming too far off the track (more than 5 meters) or not far enough (less than 3 meters).
Changing the bend at the midpoint instead of maintaining the original bend.
The loop having a sharp angle at the departure and return points instead of smooth curves.
Looking at the cone instead of ahead to the return point on the rail.
variations: Increase the depth to 10 meters, requiring a change of bend at the midpoint.
Ride a shallow loop at the canter, which introduces counter-canter in the middle portion.
Chain shallow loops on both long sides for a continuous flowing exercise.
tips: Tip: Shallow loops are excellent for introducing counter-flexion and counter-canter in a gentle way.

title: Half-Halt Timing Exercise at the Walk
steps: At the working walk, plan half-halts at every arena letter around the perimeter.
A half-halt is a brief, simultaneous action: close your fingers on the reins, engage your core, and lighten your seat.
The half-halt lasts less than one stride. Immediately soften again after the half-halt.
At each letter, execute one crisp half-halt. The horse should briefly lighten the forehand and step more under with the hind legs.
After circling the arena once with half-halts at every letter, evaluate: did each half-halt produce a response?
If the horse ignored a half-halt, make the next one slightly stronger. If it over-reacted, make it softer.
mistakes: The half-halt being too long, becoming a full halt or a significant slow-down.
Using only the reins without the seat and core, creating a pulling half-halt.
Not releasing after the half-halt, so the horse never gets the reward of softened aids.
Every half-halt being the same intensity regardless of the horse's response.
variations: Add half-halts before every transition for seamless gait changes.
Practice half-halts at the canter for three-beat timing.
Use half-halts to adjust the stride length without changing the gait.
tips: Tip: A half-halt is like tapping someone on the shoulder — brief, clear, and followed by release. It's a conversation, not a command.

title: Simple Change Through Walk at the Walk
steps: Begin at the working walk, tracking right on a 20-meter circle.
At X (or a designated point), transition from walk to walk. Allow 3-5 clear walk steps.
After the walk steps, pick up the left lead walk — the new inside lead.
Ride the 20-meter circle on the new lead for one full revolution.
At X again, walk for 3-5 steps and pick up the right lead.
Repeat the simple change 4-6 times. Gradually reduce the number of walk steps to 3.
The walk steps should be clear, forward, and balanced — not shuffling or rushing.
mistakes: The horse trotting instead of walking between canter leads.
Too many walk strides, losing the connection between the two canters.
Picking up the wrong lead on the second canter depart.
The horse becoming tense and anticipating the lead change.
variations: Perform simple changes on a figure-eight, changing lead at X.
Reduce to 2-3 walk strides for a more advanced simple change.
Use simple changes as preparation for flying changes by progressive reduction of walk strides.
tips: Tip: Count the walk steps out loud: 'one, two, three, canter.' This keeps you disciplined about the number of steps.

title: S-Curve Through Cones at the Walk
steps: Set up 6 cones in a straight line down the center of the arena, spaced about 10 meters apart.
Starting at the first cone, weave through the line in an S-curve pattern, passing each cone on alternating sides.
At the walk, focus on smooth changes of bend as you curve around each cone.
Your inside leg pushes the horse around each cone. Your outside leg supports and prevents the haunches from swinging.
Progress to the walk once the walk feels smooth. The S-curve becomes more challenging at speed.
Repeat the weave 3-4 times in each direction.
mistakes: Cutting too close to the cones and knocking them over.
Making sharp turns instead of smooth, flowing S-curves.
Not establishing the new bend early enough, resulting in flat spots between curves.
The horse rushing through the pattern instead of maintaining a steady tempo.
variations: Move the cones closer together (8 meters) for tighter turns.
Ride the S-curve at the canter for advanced suppleness work.
Add a halt at every other cone for transition practice within the pattern.
tips: Tip: Think of your horse as a river flowing between rocks. The path should be smooth and continuous, not jagged.

title: Figure-Eight with Simple Changes at the Walk
steps: Set up a figure-eight using two 20-meter circles meeting at X.
Begin at the walk on the right lead on the right circle.
As you approach X, transition to walk for 3-5 steps.
Pick up the left lead and ride the left circle at the walk.
After one full circle, return to X, walk for 3-5 steps, and pick up the right lead again.
Repeat 4-6 times. The walk steps should be clean and forward, not shuffling.
This is the fundamental simple change exercise used in Second Level dressage tests.
mistakes: Trotting instead of walking between lead changes.
Too many walk steps, losing the connection between canters.
Picking up the wrong lead after the walk transition.
The figure-eight becoming lopsided with one circle larger than the other.
variations: Reduce walk steps to 2-3 for a more advanced version.
Progress toward flying changes by eventually eliminating the walk steps entirely.
Use 15-meter circles instead of 20 for increased collection demand.
tips: Tip: Think of the walk steps as a brief rest stop, not a destination. The canter energy should stay alive through the walk.

title: Half-Pass Zigzag at Walk
steps: Start on the center line at the collected walk. Half-pass to the right, moving diagonally toward the right track.
After reaching the quarter line, straighten for 2-3 strides, then change the bend and half-pass to the left.
Continue the zigzag: half-pass right to the quarter line, straighten, half-pass left to the opposite quarter line.
Each half-pass segment should cover equal distance both forward and sideways.
The changes of bend should happen in the 2-3 straight strides. The horse must be supple enough to switch quickly.
Complete 4 direction changes (2 zigzags), then ride forward on the center line and walk.
mistakes: The segments being uneven — more sideways in one direction than the other.
The horse losing collection during the direction changes.
Not enough straight strides between half-pass directions, causing a choppy transition.
The haunches leading instead of the shoulders in the half-pass portions.
variations: Progress to half-pass zigzag at the collected canter.
Increase to 6 direction changes for a longer exercise.
Vary the width of each zigzag segment for adjustability training.
tips: Tip: The half-pass zigzag is one of the most demanding dressage movements. Master half-pass in isolation before attempting zigzags.

title: Half-Pass Zigzag at Trot at the Trot
steps: Start on the center line at the collected trot. Half-pass to the right, moving diagonally toward the right track.
After reaching the quarter line, straighten for 2-3 strides, then change the bend and half-pass to the left.
Continue the zigzag: half-pass right to the quarter line, straighten, half-pass left to the opposite quarter line.
Each half-pass segment should cover equal distance both forward and sideways.
The changes of bend should happen in the 2-3 straight strides. The horse must be supple enough to switch quickly.
Complete 4 direction changes (2 zigzags), then ride forward on the center line and trot.
mistakes: The segments being uneven — more sideways in one direction than the other.
The horse losing collection during the direction changes.
Not enough straight strides between half-pass directions, causing a choppy transition.
The haunches leading instead of the shoulders in the half-pass portions.
variations: Progress to half-pass zigzag at the collected canter.
Increase to 6 direction changes for a longer exercise.
Vary the width of each zigzag segment for adjustability training.
tips: Tip: The half-pass zigzag is one of the most demanding dressage movements. Master half-pass in isolation before attempting zigzags.

title: Half-Pass Zigzag at Canter
steps: Start on the center line at the collected canter. Half-pass to the right, moving diagonally toward the right track.
After reaching the quarter line, straighten for 2-3 strides, then change the bend and half-pass to the left.
Continue the zigzag: half-pass right to the quarter line, straighten, half-pass left to the opposite quarter line.
Each half-pass segment should cover equal distance both forward and sideways.
The changes of bend should happen in the 2-3 straight strides. The horse must be supple enough to switch quickly.
Complete 4 direction changes (2 zigzags), then ride forward on the center line and canter.
mistakes: The segments being uneven — more sideways in one direction than the other.
The horse losing collection during the direction changes.
Not enough straight strides between half-pass directions, causing a choppy transition.
The haunches leading instead of the shoulders in the half-pass portions.
variations: Progress to half-pass zigzag at the collected canter.
Increase to 6 direction changes for a longer exercise.
Vary the width of each zigzag segment for adjustability training.
tips: Tip: The half-pass zigzag is one of the most demanding dressage movements. Master half-pass in isolation before attempting zigzags.

title: Center Line Riding at the Walk
steps: At A, turn onto the center line heading toward C. Look straight ahead at C to guide your line.
Your horse should be absolutely straight on the center line. Use both legs evenly to prevent drift.
Check your straightness by glancing down at the line in the footing (if visible) or at the arena walls to ensure equal distance on both sides.
Ride the full center line at the walk. At C, turn left or right onto the track.
Repeat at the walk. Straightness becomes harder at faster gaits because any imbalance is magnified.
Practice the center line 4-6 times. This is a fundamental skill for every dressage test entry.
mistakes: Drifting to one side because the horse favors one shoulder over the other.
Looking down at the horse's neck instead of ahead at C.
The initial turn onto the center line being too shallow or too deep.
Losing straightness in the last 3-4 strides before C because the horse anticipates the turn.
variations: Add a halt at X for a halt-salute test preparation exercise.
Ride the center line in medium trot for added difficulty.
Practice the center line at the canter with a specific lead for test preparation.
tips: Tip: The center line reveals the truth about your horse's straightness. Film it from C and you'll see every drift and wobble.

title: 15-Meter Circle at the Walk
steps: Place four cones marking the quarter points of a 15-meter circle at E or B.
Pick up a working walk and begin riding the circle, aiming to pass directly over each cone.
Keep your inside leg at the girth to maintain bend. Your outside leg sits just behind the girth to prevent the haunches from swinging out.
Use your outside rein as a supporting rein to control the size of the circle. Avoid over-bending through the neck.
Ride 5 complete circles, then transition to walk and check your horse for straightness on a straight line.
Change direction and repeat on the other rein. Note which direction feels easier for your horse.
Progress by riding the circle without cones, then checking your track in the footing.
mistakes: Making an egg shape instead of a true circle because the horse drifts toward the gate.
Over-bending the neck while the shoulder falls out, creating a bulging circle.
Sitting crooked with more weight in the outside stirrup.
Rushing the tempo as the circle size decreases from 20 to 15 meters.
variations: Shrink to a 12-meter circle for 2 revolutions, then leg yield back out to 15 meters.
Add halt transitions on the circle, maintaining the curved track.
Alternate between 15-meter and 20-meter circles at the same arena letter.
tips: Tip: Think of your circle as a clock face. At each quarter point, briefly check: is my horse's body uniformly bent from poll to tail?

title: Medium Walk Across the Diagonal
steps: Establish a balanced working walk. Prepare for the diagonal line.
At the corner letter, turn across the diagonal. In the first 3-4 strides, ask the horse to lengthen into medium walk.
Allow the stride to grow longer and more ground-covering. The tempo stays the same — only the stride length increases.
Your posting should become bigger and more forward to match the longer stride.
Maintain the medium walk across the diagonal until 3-4 strides before the track.
Collect back to working walk before reaching the track. The collection should be smooth, not abrupt.
Change direction and repeat on the other diagonal.
mistakes: The horse running faster instead of lengthening the stride.
Losing balance and falling onto the forehand during the medium trot.
Not collecting soon enough and arriving at the track unbalanced.
The rider gripping with the knees and blocking the horse's shoulder movement.
variations: Perform medium trot on the long side for a different spatial challenge.
Add a 10-meter circle at X in the working trot before continuing medium trot.
Progress to extended trot across the diagonal for even greater lengthening.
tips: Tip: Medium trot should feel like the horse is pushing more from behind, not pulling from the front. The engine is in the hindquarters.

title: Medium Walk on the Long Side
steps: Establish a balanced working walk on the track. Sit deep and maintain a steady rhythm.
At the beginning of the long side, allow the walk to open up into medium walk.
Your hips follow the bigger walk stride. Soften your hands forward slightly to allow the horse to lengthen the frame.
The medium walk should cover more ground per stride without speeding up the tempo.
Maintain medium walk for the length of the long side.
Before the corner, collect back to working walk using half-halts from your core and outside rein.
The transition back to working walk should happen over 3-4 strides, not abruptly.
mistakes: The horse flattening and running instead of truly lengthening the canter stride.
Not collecting soon enough, causing a scrambled turn through the corner.
Losing the three-beat canter rhythm in the medium — it becomes four-beat.
Leaning back too much in the collection, pulling on the horse's mouth.
variations: Perform medium canter across the diagonal for a different spatial challenge.
Alternate medium and collected canter every half of the long side.
Progress to extended canter for maximum ground-coverage.
tips: Tip: Think of medium canter as turning up the volume on a speaker — same song, just bigger. The rhythm doesn't change.
