Perform on a 15-meter circle to increase the balance demand.
tips: Tip: If the horse picks up the wrong lead, calmly trot and try again. Don't punish, just redirect.

title: Extended Trot Development at the Trot
steps: Begin with a working trot, then collect for 8-10 strides to store energy in the hindquarters.
At the beginning of the diagonal, release that stored energy: allow the horse to push powerfully from behind.
The extended trot should show maximum ground cover with each stride. The legs should reach forward, not just up.
Your posting becomes bigger and slower to match the longer stride. Let your hands follow the head motion.
Maintain the extension across the full diagonal, keeping the rhythm steady.
Before the track, collect firmly over 4-5 strides. Don't slam on the brakes.
Trot on a long rein and assess: did the horse push more from behind or just run faster?
mistakes: Confusing extension with speed — the horse should lengthen the stride, not quicken the tempo.
Falling onto the forehand because the horse is pushing flat instead of uphill.
Losing the collection at the end, arriving at the track unbalanced.
Tension in the horse's back causing short, tight steps instead of sweeping extensions.
variations: Practice half-arena extensions: lengthen for half the diagonal, collect for the other half.
Compare your extension left vs. right — most horses extend better in one direction.
Video yourself to check if the diagonal pairs of legs reach equally (true extension) or if one pair is lazy.
tips: Tip: True extension comes from collection. You cannot extend what you haven't first compressed. Always collect before you extend.

title: Counting Strides Between Letters at the Walk
steps: At the working walk, count the number of strides from one letter to the next along the long side.
Ride the long side and count: H to E might be 12 strides, for example.
Now ride the same long side and try to fit exactly 12 strides again. Same rhythm, same stride length.
Change it: try to fit 14 strides (shorter, more collected) or 10 strides (longer, more extended).
Ride back and forth, changing the stride count target each time.
This exercise teaches you to feel and control stride length, which is essential for jumping and dressage.
mistakes: Losing count because the rider stops focusing mid-way.
Changing the tempo instead of the stride length to hit the count.
Only being able to lengthen but not shorten (or vice versa).
Counting every stride of the posting trot instead of every other one (posting trot has two phases per stride).
variations: Count strides on a diagonal and adjust: lengthen or shorten to hit a target number.
Apply the counting exercise at the canter for jumping preparation.
Count strides in a combination: letter A to B = 12, letter B to C = 14 (different stride lengths).
tips: Tip: This skill directly transfers to jumping distances. If you can adjust flatwork strides on demand, you can ride any distance to a fence.

title: Counting Strides Between Letters at the Trot
steps: At the working trot, count the number of strides from one letter to the next along the long side.
Ride the long side and count: H to E might be 12 strides, for example.
Now ride the same long side and try to fit exactly 12 strides again. Same rhythm, same stride length.
Change it: try to fit 14 strides (shorter, more collected) or 10 strides (longer, more extended).
Ride back and forth, changing the stride count target each time.
This exercise teaches you to feel and control stride length, which is essential for jumping and dressage.
mistakes: Losing count because the rider stops focusing mid-way.
Changing the tempo instead of the stride length to hit the count.
Only being able to lengthen but not shorten (or vice versa).
Counting every stride of the posting trot instead of every other one (posting trot has two phases per stride).
variations: Count strides on a diagonal and adjust: lengthen or shorten to hit a target number.
Apply the counting exercise at the canter for jumping preparation.
Count strides in a combination: letter A to B = 12, letter B to C = 14 (different stride lengths).
tips: Tip: This skill directly transfers to jumping distances. If you can adjust flatwork strides on demand, you can ride any distance to a fence.

title: Trot Poles in a Straight Line
steps: Set up 4 ground poles in a straight line, spaced 4.5 feet apart (one normal trot stride).
Approach at the posting trot on a straight line. Look ahead past the poles, not down at them.
Allow the horse to find its rhythm over the poles. Keep a steady contact but don't interfere.
The horse should trot through without speeding up, slowing down, or jumping the poles.
After the poles, continue trotting in a straight line for 6-8 strides before turning.
Repeat 4-6 times from each direction. The horse should become more confident with each pass.
mistakes: Looking down at the poles instead of ahead, which drops the horse onto the forehand.
Pulling on the reins as the horse approaches, causing it to shorten its stride and hit the poles.
Letting the horse rush through the poles — maintain the same trot tempo before, during, and after.
Leaning forward over the poles, getting ahead of the horse's motion.
variations: Increase to 6 poles for a longer exercise.
Raise alternate poles on one end (1-2 inches) to encourage the horse to lift its legs higher.
Fan the poles on a curve to practice trot poles on a bending line.
tips: Tip: Let the horse figure out the poles. Your job is to maintain rhythm and direction. The horse will learn to adjust its stride.

title: Canter Poles in a Line
steps: Set up 4 ground poles spaced 9 feet apart (one normal canter stride between each).
Approach at the working canter. Establish a steady rhythm 6-8 strides before the poles.
Look ahead past the poles. Keep your leg on to maintain impulsion through the poles.
The horse should canter through with one stride between each pole without chipping in extra strides.
After the poles, maintain the canter for 6-8 strides before transitioning or turning.
Repeat from both leads. Note if the horse is more comfortable on one lead.
mistakes: The horse breaking to trot before or during the poles.
Chipping in an extra stride between poles because the canter is too short.
The horse jumping the poles instead of cantering over them.
The rider throwing their upper body forward at each pole.
variations: Increase the spacing to 10 feet to encourage a longer stride.
Decrease to 8 feet to develop collection and adjustability.
Raise the poles to 6 inches to create low cavaletti work.
tips: Tip: If the horse adds strides, the canter is too slow. If it leaves out strides, the canter is too fast. Adjust the canter, not the pole spacing.

title: Fan Poles on a Circle
steps: Set up 4 poles in a fan shape on a 20-meter circle. The inside end should be 3.5 feet apart, the outside end about 5.5 feet.
At the trot, ride the circle and trot through the fan of poles.
If you ride toward the inside of the poles (narrower spacing), the stride will be shorter.
If you ride toward the outside (wider spacing), the stride will be longer.
Practice riding through the middle first, then adjust inside or outside to change the stride.
Maintain the bend throughout. The horse should stay on the circle path through the poles.
mistakes: The horse straightening through the poles instead of maintaining the circle bend.
Riding too close to the inside (narrow end) and the horse tripping on closely-spaced poles.
Not maintaining a consistent path through all four poles.
Losing rhythm as the horse encounters the poles.
variations: Canter through the fan poles on a circle at 9-foot outside spacing.
Use 6 poles for a longer fan exercise.
Combine with a jump at the end of the fan: poles on the circle leading to a small fence.
tips: Tip: Fan poles teach adjustability without you having to move poles between exercises. Just change your path through them.

title: Bounce Poles at the Trot
steps: Set up 3 ground poles spaced 3 feet apart (bounce distance at the trot).
Approach at a steady sitting trot. The horse will take quick, bouncy steps over each pole.
Keep your contact steady and your upper body slightly behind vertical to support the horse.
The horse should not have a full stride between poles — it bounces from one to the next.
This exercise improves the horse's hind leg engagement and front leg technique.
Repeat 4-6 times. The horse should get smoother with each pass.
mistakes: Approaching too fast, causing the horse to jump the poles instead of bouncing.
The horse stopping or running out because the close spacing surprises it.
The rider getting ahead of the motion and landing on the horse's neck.
Not enough impulsion, causing the horse to shuffle through lazily.
variations: Increase to 4-5 bounce poles for a longer exercise.
Raise the poles to cavaletti height (6-8 inches) for more engagement.
Follow the bounce poles with a small cross-rail 9 feet after the last pole.
tips: Tip: Bounce poles are gymnastics for horses. They develop the quick reflexes and hind leg engagement needed for jumping.

title: Raised Trot Poles
steps: Set up 4 trot poles raised 4-6 inches off the ground on cavaletti blocks.
Space them 4.5 feet apart (standard trot stride distance).
Approach at the working trot. The raised height encourages the horse to lift its legs higher.
Maintain a steady rhythm. Allow the horse to use its body by keeping a soft, following contact.
The horse should round its back and engage its abdominals as it steps over the raised poles.
Repeat 4-6 times. This is excellent for building topline strength.
mistakes: Poles set too high for the horse's current fitness level, causing it to jump instead of trot.
The horse rushing through because the raised poles are intimidating.
Inconsistent spacing causing the horse to scramble over the last pole.
The rider standing up in the stirrups instead of maintaining a normal posting trot.
variations: Raise only alternate poles to add variety.
Increase to 6 raised poles for a longer strengthening exercise.
Set the poles on a curve for combined bending and strengthening work.
tips: Tip: Raised poles are one of the best tools for building topline muscle and teaching the horse to use its body efficiently over fences.

title: One-Stride Pole Exercise
steps: Set up 2 ground poles spaced 18 feet apart (one canter stride between them).
Approach at the canter. The horse should land after the first pole, take one canter stride, and step over the second pole.
Focus on maintaining a steady canter rhythm. Don't adjust the horse's stride — let the poles teach.
If the horse chips in an extra stride, the canter is too slow. Approach with more impulsion next time.
If the horse gets too close to the second pole, the canter is too fast. Half-halt on approach.
Repeat 4-6 times from each direction until the one-stride feels effortless.
mistakes: Adding an extra stride because the canter lacks impulsion.
The horse jumping both poles in one big leap instead of striding between them.
The rider interfering by pulling or pushing at the wrong moment.
Not approaching on a straight line, causing the horse to drift sideways.
variations: Change the distance to 21 feet (one longer stride) to practice lengthening.
Add a third pole at 18 feet for a two-stride exercise.
Replace the ground poles with small cross-rails for a one-stride jumping exercise.
tips: Tip: Pole distances teach your eye. Once you can see a one-stride from 6 strides away, you're developing the feel for related distances.

title: Ground Pole Grid: Four-Stride Line
steps: Set up 2 ground poles with 60 feet between them (4 normal canter strides).
Place cones alongside to mark a straight track.
Approach at the working canter. Count your strides: 1-2-3-4. The horse should arrive at the second pole in exactly 4 strides.
If you get 5 strides, open your horse's stride next time. If you get 3, collect more.
Now try to fit 5 strides in the same distance (shorter, more collected strides).
Then try 3 strides (longer, more open strides).
This adjustability is the foundation of all course riding.
mistakes: Counting strides wrong — start counting when the horse lands after the first pole.
Pulling on the reins to add strides instead of using your seat and half-halts.
Kicking to remove strides instead of opening your body and allowing a bigger canter.
Not approaching on a straight line, making the distance longer or shorter than intended.
variations: Set up two different four-stride lines and ride them on a course.
Add cross-rails instead of ground poles for a more realistic exercise.
Practice the exercise counting down: 4-3-2-1 to develop awareness of where each stride falls.
tips: Tip: Four strides is the most common related distance in show jumping. Master this and you can ride any course.

title: Trot Pole Serpentine
steps: Set up 3 pairs of trot poles scattered across the arena, each pair spaced 4.5 feet apart.
Design a serpentine path that connects all three pairs with smooth curves.
Trot the serpentine, passing through each pair of poles on the bending line.
Maintain the trot rhythm through each pair and through each turn.
The turns between pole pairs develop the horse's ability to change bend while maintaining focus.
Repeat the serpentine 3-4 times, then reverse the direction for practice on the other rein.
mistakes: Cutting the turns too tight between pole pairs.
The horse speeding up between poles in anticipation.
Losing the trot rhythm over the poles because the approach angle isn't straight enough.
Not planning the turns far enough in advance.
variations: Raise the poles to cavaletti height for increased difficulty.
Add small cross-rails instead of ground poles.
Canter the serpentine with simple lead changes between pole pairs.
tips: Tip: Think of this as a mini-course without the jumps. The skills you develop here — turn planning, rhythm, line accuracy — directly transfer to course riding.

title: Bounce Grid: Two Cross-Rails
steps: Set up 2 small cross-rails 9-10 feet apart (bounce distance).
Approach at the trot. The horse will jump the first cross-rail, land, and immediately jump the second.
There is no stride between the fences — the horse bounces from one to the next.
Keep your upper body slightly behind vertical and maintain a steady leg contact.
Allow your arms to follow the horse's mouth over each jump. Don't drop the contact.
Repeat 4-6 times. Start with the cross-rails very low (12 inches) and gradually raise them.
mistakes: Approaching too fast, causing the horse to jump too big and land too far into the bounce.
The rider getting left behind on the first jump and interfering with the second.
Not enough impulsion, causing the horse to stop between the two fences.
Leaning too far forward, putting weight on the horse's forehand.
variations: Add a third cross-rail for a three-element bounce.
Raise the fences to verticals at 2 feet for more effort.
Place a ground pole one stride (18 feet) before the bounce as a placing pole.
tips: Tip: Bounces are the best gymnastic exercise. They teach the horse to use its body correctly without relying on speed.

title: One-Stride Gymnastic
steps: Set up a placing pole, then a cross-rail 9 feet later, then a second cross-rail 18 feet after the first (one stride).
Approach at the trot. The placing pole helps the horse arrive at the correct takeoff distance.
The horse jumps the first cross-rail, lands, takes one canter stride, and jumps the second.
Your job is to maintain balance and rhythm. Don't interfere with the horse's natural arc.
Keep your eyes up and your leg on. Allow the horse to figure out the striding.
Start low (12 inches) and gradually raise to 18 inches as the horse gains confidence.
mistakes: Looking down at the fences instead of up and ahead.
Pulling on the reins between the two fences, shortening the horse's stride.
Throwing the upper body forward over each jump, getting ahead of the motion.
Approaching in canter instead of trot, which changes the distance.
variations: Add a third fence at 18 feet for a two-stride gymnastic.
Change the distances: 17 feet for a shorter stride, 19 feet for a longer stride.
Raise to verticals at 2-2.5 feet for more challenge.
tips: Tip: Gymnastics teach your horse to jump correctly without you having to micromanage the takeoff. Trust the exercise.

title: Bounce to One-Stride Combination
steps: Set up: placing pole, then cross-rail, then 10 feet to a second cross-rail (bounce), then 18 feet to a vertical (one stride).
Approach at the trot. The placing pole sets up the first jump.
Bounce the first two fences, then the horse has one stride to the third fence.
The bounce develops quickness; the one-stride develops scope and bascule.
Repeat 4-6 times, gradually raising the fences as the horse gains confidence.
If the horse struggles, lower everything and rebuild confidence.
mistakes: The horse rushing after the bounce, arriving too close to the third fence.
Not enough impulsion to clear the third fence after the demanding bounce.
The rider getting ahead of the motion in the bounce and interfering with the one-stride.
Setting the fences too high before the horse is ready for the combination.
variations: Extend to bounce-one stride-one stride for a longer exercise.
Add an oxer as the final fence for scope development.
Change the one-stride distance to test adjustability: 17 feet vs. 19 feet.
tips: Tip: This is a classic gymnastic combination. The bounce teaches quick feet; the one-stride teaches the horse to push and stretch.

title: Three-Element Bounce Grid
steps: Set up 3 cross-rails, each 10 feet apart (bounce-bounce).
Approach at the trot. The horse bounces through all three fences without a stride between any of them.
This is physically demanding. Start at very low heights (12 inches) and only raise when the horse is comfortable.
Keep your upper body centered and your leg secure. Your hands must follow the horse's mouth over each fence.
The horse will develop explosive hind leg power and quick front leg folding from this exercise.
Limit to 4-5 passes per session. Bounces are hard work.
mistakes: The horse losing momentum and stopping at the second or third element.
Setting the fences too high for a three-bounce, causing the horse to struggle.
The rider getting bounced out of the saddle because of the rapid succession.
The horse drifting sideways through the grid.
variations: Progress to a four-element bounce for advanced horses.
Mix cross-rails and verticals: cross-rail, vertical, cross-rail.
Add a one-stride after the final bounce to a slightly larger fence.
tips: Tip: Three bounces is the sweet spot — enough repetition to build technique without being so long that the horse loses energy.

title: Gymnastic Line: Trot In, Canter Out
steps: Set up: trot pole at 8 feet, cross-rail, 18 feet to a vertical, 19 feet to a slightly larger vertical.
Approach at the trot. The trot pole ensures a correct takeoff to the first fence.
The horse will canter after the first fence. Use the one-stride distances to maintain rhythm.
Each fence can be slightly larger than the previous one, creating a building exercise.
Keep your eyes up and your leg active. Let the gymnastic do the training.
The final fence can be raised 3-6 inches higher than the others to develop scope.
mistakes: Approaching in canter instead of trot, which throws off the first distance.
Interfering with the horse's canter between fences.
Getting ahead of the motion and weighing down the forehand.
Raising the fences too quickly before the horse builds confidence.
variations: Add a fourth element at one stride to extend the gymnastic.
Replace the final fence with an oxer for scope development.
Add a bending line after the gymnastic to a single fence for course simulation.
tips: Tip: Gymnastics are the most efficient way to improve a horse's jumping technique. 15 minutes of grid work equals an hour of course practice.

title: Two-Jump Bending Line
steps: Set up two small verticals about 60 feet apart on a bending line (not a straight line).
The rider must arc between the two fences, choosing the path and number of strides.
A direct line (less curve) might ride in 4 strides. A bending line (more curve) might ride in 5.
Approach the first fence on a good canter. After landing, look at the second fence and plan your curve.
Use your inside leg to maintain impulsion on the curve. Your outside rein guides the track.
Practice both the 4-stride (direct) and 5-stride (bending) option to develop adjustability.
mistakes: Not planning the line before jumping the first fence.
Drifting wide on the bending line, adding too many strides.
Cutting the line too tight and getting too close to the second fence.
Losing impulsion on the curve because the rider focuses only on direction.
variations: Set up the line with different options: 4, 5, or 6 strides depending on the path.
Ride the bending line in both directions for a figure-eight course.
Add a third fence after the bending line for course simulation.
tips: Tip: Bending lines test your track-planning skills. Walk the line on foot first to see the different paths available.

title: Simple Course: Figure-Eight Pattern
steps: Set up 2 small fences, one on each 20-meter circle of a figure-eight pattern.
Start by cantering a circle over one fence, then change direction through the center to jump the second fence.
After each fence, establish a balanced canter on the circle before approaching the next.
The change of direction in the middle requires a simple change or a flying change.
Ride the figure-eight 3-4 times, focusing on smooth changes of lead and consistent approaches.
Each approach to a fence should feel the same: balanced canter, steady rhythm, looking ahead.
mistakes: Rushing to the next fence without establishing a balanced canter first.
Forgetting to change leads, jumping the second fence on the wrong lead.
The turns becoming too tight or too wide, affecting the approach.
Losing confidence and checking (pulling) on the approach.
variations: Add a third fence in the middle of the figure-eight for a three-jump course.
Raise the fences to test confidence at height.
Ride the figure-eight in a specific stride count for precision.
tips: Tip: The figure-eight is the simplest course pattern, but it tests everything: lead changes, approaches, rhythm, and track planning.

title: Rollback Turn to a Single Fence
steps: Set up a single vertical in the center of the arena.
Jump the fence, then immediately ride a tight rollback turn (half-circle) and jump it again from the other direction.
The rollback should be a smooth half-circle, not a sharp pivot. Maintain the canter throughout.
Start with a wide rollback (20-meter half-circle) and gradually tighten to 10-15 meters.
The horse needs to land, rebalance on the turn, and set up for the next approach in just a few strides.
Practice rollbacks to both directions for even development.
mistakes: The turn being too tight, causing the horse to break to trot or lose balance.
Not looking early enough in the direction of the rollback turn.
Landing and running straight for too many strides before starting the turn.
The second approach being rushed and unbalanced.
variations: Set up two fences and alternate rollback turns between them.
Tighten the rollback to simulate jump-off turns.
Add a related distance after the rollback: rollback to two-stride line.
tips: Tip: Rollbacks win jump-offs. The tighter your rollback, the fewer strides you waste. But tightness should never sacrifice balance.

title: Inside Turn Between Two Fences
steps: Set up two fences facing the same direction, about 40 feet apart laterally.
Jump the first fence, then ride an inside turn (toward the center of the arena) to approach the second fence.
The inside turn saves time compared to going wide around the end of the arena.
You must land, look immediately to the inside, half-halt, and bend the horse toward the second fence.
Start with a wider inside turn and gradually tighten as you and your horse gain confidence.
Practice turning to both the left and right for even development.
mistakes: Starting the turn too late, running past the second fence.
The turn being so tight that the horse loses balance and chips in extra strides.
Not looking at the second fence soon enough — your eye directs the turn.
Landing on the wrong lead, making the inside turn difficult.
variations: Set up 4 fences in a box and practice inside turns between each pair.
Alternate inside and outside turns to the same fences.
Time yourself to see how much faster the inside turn is versus going wide.
tips: Tip: Inside turns are all about your eye. Look at the second fence before you leave the ground over the first. Your body will follow your eyes.

title: Four-Fence Mini Course
steps: Set up 4 small fences in a pattern that requires turns and changes of direction.
Walk the course on foot first: plan your track, count your strides between fences, identify your lead changes.
Start with fence 1. After landing, plan your turn to fence 2. Focus on a balanced approach.
After fence 2, adjust your canter for the distance to fence 3. Look ahead to plan your track.
Fence 3 to fence 4 completes the course. Finish with a controlled canter after the last fence.
Debrief: were your distances right? Were your turns smooth? Were your leads correct?
Repeat the course 2-3 times, improving each element with every pass.
mistakes: Not walking the course on foot before riding it.
Getting lost mid-course because you didn't memorize the pattern.
Rushing between fences instead of re-establishing rhythm.
Only focusing on the current fence instead of planning 2 fences ahead.
variations: Raise the fences for confidence building at height.
Add a fifth and sixth fence for a more complex course.
Time the course and try to ride it more efficiently (not faster, but smoother).
tips: Tip: Always think two fences ahead. While you're jumping fence 1, you should already be planning fence 2 and looking toward fence 3.

title: Canter Adjustability Between Poles
steps: Set up 2 ground poles 72 feet apart (approximately 5 normal canter strides).
Approach at the working canter. Count your strides between the poles: you should get 5.
On the next pass, try to fit 6 strides (collected, shorter canter).
On the third pass, try to fit 4 strides (open, longer canter without running).
The goal is to ride the same distance in different stride counts by adjusting stride length.
Practice until you can reliably hit 4, 5, or 6 strides on demand.
mistakes: Changing the tempo (speed) instead of the stride length to adjust the count.
Not committing to the adjustment — half-hearted collection that doesn't change the count.
Pulling on the reins to add strides instead of using seat and half-halts.
Running at the poles to leave out strides, arriving dangerously close to the second pole.
variations: Change the distance to 60 feet for a 4-stride normal, 3/5 stride adjustability exercise.
Add a third pole for two consecutive distance questions.
Replace poles with small fences for real jumping adjustability.
tips: Tip: Stride adjustability is the single most important skill in jumping. A rider who can add or leave out a stride can ride any course.

title: Canter-Halt-Canter on a Straight Line
steps: Set up 4 cones in a line, 60 feet apart, along the long side.
Canter from cone 1 to cone 2. At cone 2, halt. Stand for 3 seconds.
From the halt, immediately pick up canter to cone 3. Halt at cone 3.
Continue the canter-halt-canter pattern through all 4 cones.
The halts should be prompt and square. The canter departures should be clean and on the correct lead.
This exercise develops the responsiveness needed for adjustability in jumping courses.
mistakes: Trotting between the halt and canter instead of making clean transitions.
The halts being messy — the horse walking forward or swinging sideways.
Losing the correct lead on the canter departures.
The canter becoming tense and rushed from the repeated halts.
variations: Reduce to 3 cones for a simpler version.
Replace halts with walk transitions (3 walk steps) for an easier version.
Add this exercise before a gymnastics session to sharpen the horse's responsiveness.
tips: Tip: If your horse can halt cleanly from canter and depart cleanly from halt, it has the obedience to handle any distance question in a course.

title: Counter-Canter for Jumpers
steps: Establish a balanced canter on the correct lead on a 20-meter circle.
Change direction through the center of the arena without changing leads.
Now you're in counter-canter on the new circle. Maintain the lead for 1-2 full circles.
The horse should stay balanced and rhythmic in counter-canter.
This develops the horse's ability to hold a lead when asked — essential for inside turns and related distances.
Return to true canter via a simple change and practice on the other lead.
mistakes: The horse swapping leads automatically because it hasn't been taught to hold the counter-canter.
Over-bending toward the lead, destabilizing the balance.
The canter becoming four-beat from tension.
The rider gripping and creating tension that causes the lead swap.
variations: Ride counter-canter on a straight line (long side) before attempting circles.
Counter-canter through a turn to a single fence for jump-off preparation.
Chain true canter and counter-canter loops for advanced balance.
tips: Tip: Counter-canter teaches the horse to wait for your aid before changing leads. This is critical for jump-off strategy.

title: Jumping Position at the Trot and Canter
steps: At the trot, rise into two-point position: your seat lifts slightly off the saddle, weight drops into your heels.
Hold the two-point position for 10 strides, then sit for 10 strides. Alternate.
Your hands should stay steady over the horse's withers. Don't brace on the neck.
Progress to the canter in two-point. This is harder because of the rocking motion.
Hold two-point at the canter for 10 strides, then sit. Build up to 20, then 30 strides.
This develops the leg strength and balance needed for jumping.
mistakes: Gripping with the knees instead of keeping weight in the heels.
Resting hands on the horse's neck for balance instead of being self-supporting.
Collapsing the upper body forward instead of maintaining a flat back with eyes up.
Holding breath — keep breathing normally throughout.
variations: Hold two-point for an entire trot circle (30+ strides) for endurance.
Practice two-point while trotting over ground poles.
Add arm exercises in two-point: arms out to the side, arms forward, hands on helmet.
tips: Tip: Two-point is the foundation of jumping position. If you can't hold it for 30 seconds at the canter, you're not ready for courses.

title: Gallop Position and Pace Control
steps: In a large arena or field, establish a canter. Rise into gallop position (short stirrups, upper body forward, weight in heels).
Gallop for a set distance (between two cones), then collect to a working canter.
The transition from gallop to working canter should be smooth: close your hip angle, sit deeper, half-halt.
Practice opening and closing the gallop 3-4 times down the long side.
This simulates the pace control needed on cross-country courses.
Always maintain control. A controlled gallop is a gear you choose, not a speed you can't stop.
mistakes: Letting the gallop get away from you — always be able to stop within 6 strides.
Pulling on the horse's mouth to slow down instead of using your body.
Rising too far out of the saddle, losing lower leg contact.
Not practicing the collect-back portion enough — it's the harder skill.
variations: Set up cones at specific points for gallop-to-canter transitions.
Practice galloping to a single fence for cross-country simulation.
Time yourself between two points at gallop and working canter to feel the speed difference.
tips: Tip: A good gallop feels fast but controlled. If you can't collect at any moment, you're going too fast.

title: Seeing the Distance: Single Fence Approach
steps: Set up a single small cross-rail (12-18 inches). Approach at the working canter.
Focus on maintaining a steady rhythm — don't try to 'find' the distance by adjusting at the last moment.
Look at the top of the fence from 6 strides away. Keep your leg on and your rhythm steady.
If you arrive at a good distance, great. If it's slightly long or short, that's okay — the horse will adjust.
After each approach, evaluate: did the horse jump comfortably? Was the rhythm consistent?
Repeat 6-8 times. The goal is consistency of approach, not perfection of distance.
mistakes: Staring at the base of the fence, which drops the horse onto the forehand.
Making a major adjustment (pulling or kicking) in the last 2 strides.
Holding your breath on the approach, creating tension in your body.
Speed-up or slow-down approaches that change the rhythm in the last 3 strides.
variations: Approach from different angles: straight, slightly right, slightly left.
Close your eyes for 2 strides on the approach to develop feel.
Add a placing pole 9 feet before the fence to build distance confidence.
tips: Tip: The secret to seeing distances is not seeing them — it's maintaining rhythm. A steady canter produces good distances naturally.

title: Placing Pole to a Single Fence
steps: Set up a small vertical with a placing pole 9 feet in front of it (one trot stride).
Approach at the trot. The placing pole ensures you arrive at the correct takeoff distance every time.
Trot over the placing pole. The horse will naturally take one stride and jump the fence.
Focus on your position: heels down, eyes up, following hands, soft seat.
The consistency of the placing pole allows you to work on your form without worrying about the distance.
Repeat 6-8 times, gradually raising the fence as confidence grows.
mistakes: Approaching in canter instead of trot, which changes the placing pole distance.
Looking down at the placing pole instead of at the fence.
Tensing up and grabbing the mane over the fence.
Not keeping enough leg on the approach, causing the horse to stop.
variations: Move the placing pole to 18 feet for a canter approach with one stride.
Remove the placing pole after several successful attempts to test your independent judgment.
Add a second fence one stride after the first for a gymnastic sequence.
tips: Tip: Placing poles are training wheels for distance. Use them liberally, then gradually remove them as your eye develops.

title: Long Approach to a Single Fence
steps: Set up a single fence in the center of the arena. Your approach will be the full length of the arena.
Pick up the canter at the far end of the arena, 80-100 feet from the fence.
Establish your rhythm in the first 3-4 strides. Then maintain that exact rhythm all the way to the fence.
Do not adjust in the last 3 strides. Trust the rhythm to produce a good distance.
After jumping, canter away and evaluate: were the first 3 strides the same as the last 3?
Repeat 5-6 times. Consistency is the goal.
mistakes: Starting to 'ride the distance' from too far away, making adjustments throughout.
The horse speeding up as it approaches the fence because it can see it for a long time.
The rider getting tense and picking up the reins in the last 5 strides.
Not committing to the canter rhythm and constantly second-guessing.
variations: Practice the long approach from both directions to test both leads.
Have someone count your strides out loud to check rhythm consistency.
Use the long approach to a line: single fence then 4 strides to a second fence.
tips: Tip: The long approach tests your mental discipline. The temptation to adjust grows with every stride. Trust your rhythm.

title: Angled Approach to a Fence
steps: Set up a single vertical. Place cones to mark an approach line that angles across the fence (not perpendicular).
The angle should be mild — about 15-20 degrees off straight.
Approach on the angled line at the canter. Keep the horse straight on YOUR line, not the fence's line.
The horse should jump at an angle, landing on the same angled track on the other side.
Your outside rein and leg keep the horse from drifting to jump the fence straight.
Start with a small fence and mild angle. Gradually increase the angle as confidence builds.
mistakes: The horse drifting to jump the fence straight instead of on the angled path.
Not enough supporting outside aids, allowing the horse to run out or drift.
The approach becoming crooked — the horse should be straight on your line.
Jumping the fence at too sharp an angle, risking a run-out or stop.
variations: Practice angled approaches from both sides of the fence.
Set up two fences and practice an angled approach to one that leads to a straight approach on the other.
Use angle fences as part of a course for jump-off practice.
tips: Tip: Angled fences appear in every jump-off. Master them by starting with a small angle and building up gradually.

title: Two-Stride Related Distance
steps: Set up 2 fences 36 feet apart (two normal canter strides).
Approach at the working canter. After the first fence, the horse should land and take exactly 2 strides to the second.
Keep your leg on between the fences. Look up at the second fence, not down.
If the horse adds a third stride, approach with more impulsion next time.
If the horse gets to the second fence in one big stride, collect on the approach.
Practice until 2 strides feels automatic.
mistakes: Panicking between the fences and pulling the horse back.
Not enough impulsion, causing a chip-in third stride.
The horse jumping flat over the first fence, landing too far into the line.
The rider interfering with the horse's natural stride between fences.
variations: Try 35 feet for a steady 2 strides and 37 feet for a forward 2 strides.
Add a third fence at 36 feet for a 2-stride to 2-stride combination.
Ride the 2-stride in 3 strides (collected) for advanced adjustability.
tips: Tip: Two strides is the most common combination distance. If you can ride it cleanly every time, you can handle anything the course designer throws at you.

title: Three-Stride Related Distance
steps: Set up 2 small fences 48 feet apart (three normal canter strides).
Approach at the working canter. After the first fence, count your strides: 1-2-3.
You should arrive at the second fence with a comfortable distance on stride 3.
Keep your leg on and your eyes up. Let the canter rhythm carry you to the fence.
If you get 4 strides, your canter is too slow. If you get 2, it's too fast.
Repeat 4-6 times until the 3-stride line feels automatic.
mistakes: Counting strides wrong — start counting when the horse's front feet land after the first fence.
Changing the canter speed between the fences instead of maintaining rhythm.
Looking down between the fences instead of at the second fence.
Tensing up after the first fence because you're worried about the distance.
variations: Try the 3-stride in 4 strides (collected) or 2 strides (open) for adjustability.
Add a third fence for a 3-stride to 3-stride exercise.
Approach from trot for a different first-fence dynamic.
tips: Tip: Three strides gives you plenty of time to adjust. If you maintain rhythm, the distance takes care of itself.

title: Cross-Rail Introduction
steps: Set up a small cross-rail at 12 inches at the center (the lowest point).
Approach at the trot. Look over the fence, not at it.
The X shape of the cross-rail guides the horse to the center of the jump.
Keep your leg on all the way to the fence. Allow your hands to follow the horse's neck forward over the jump.
Land and continue in a straight line for 6-8 strides before turning.
Repeat 4-6 times. Cross-rails build confidence because the horse can see exactly where to jump.
mistakes: Pulling on the reins in the last stride, discouraging the horse from jumping.
Leaning forward before the horse takes off, getting ahead of the motion.
Not keeping enough leg, allowing the horse to stop or run out.
Looking down at the cross-rail instead of over it.
variations: Approach from the canter for a different feel.
Add a ground pole 9 feet before the cross-rail as a placing pole.
Progress to a small vertical (straight rail at 18 inches) once confident.
tips: Tip: Cross-rails are the safest first fence. The X shape invites the horse to the center and makes the jump feel small.

title: Vertical Jump: Building Height
steps: Start with a vertical at 2 feet. Approach at the working canter.
Maintain rhythm and impulsion. The horse needs to use its hind legs to clear a vertical.
After 3 successful jumps at 2 feet, raise to 2'3". Repeat 3 clean jumps.
Continue raising in 3-inch increments: 2'6", 2'9", 3'0" (if your level permits).
At each height, evaluate: is the horse jumping cleanly? Is it rocking back on the haunches for takeoff?
Stop at the first sign of struggling or loss of form. Quality over height.
mistakes: Raising the fence before the horse is consistently clean at the current height.
Getting greedy with height and pushing past the horse's current ability.
The horse hanging its front legs because it's reaching instead of jumping with technique.
The rider getting behind the motion as the fences get bigger.
variations: Add a placing pole to maintain consistent takeoff distances.
Compare the horse's technique over a vertical vs. an oxer at the same height.
Use a gymnastic approach (trot in to one-stride) for height building.
tips: Tip: Height comes from technique, not courage. A horse that uses its body correctly at 2 feet will be safe at 3 feet.

title: Oxer Introduction
steps: Set up a small ascending oxer: front rail at 18 inches, back rail at 2 feet, with a 12-inch spread.
The ascending shape (lower in front, higher in back) helps the horse judge the width.
Approach at the working canter with strong impulsion. Oxers require more forward energy than verticals.
The horse needs to jump both up AND across. Your leg must stay active to the fence.
Allow your hands to follow the horse's neck. Oxers produce a rounder, more forward jump arc.
Repeat 4-5 times, then gradually widen the spread by 3-6 inches.
mistakes: Not enough impulsion, causing the horse to jump flat and possibly hit the back rail.
Pulling on the reins at the last moment, which shortens the horse's stride and causes a poor takeoff.
Setting the spread too wide before the horse is comfortable with the concept.
The rider not following the horse's motion over the wider jump.
variations: Progress from ascending oxer to square oxer (both rails the same height).
Add an oxer to a gymnastic line as the final fence.
Practice oxers on bending lines for course simulation.
tips: Tip: Oxers need forward energy. Ride them with more leg than a vertical. The horse needs to push off the ground with power to clear the width.

title: Combination: In-and-Out
steps: Set up two small fences 24 feet apart (one stride). This is an in-and-out combination.
Approach at the working canter. After the first fence, the horse has exactly one stride before the second.
Keep your leg on between the fences. The horse should jump in, stride, and jump out smoothly.
Your upper body should stay centered — don't lean forward between the fences.
Practice from both directions (the distances may feel different depending on the horse).
This is a standard combination found in almost every jumping course.
mistakes: The horse adding a stride (getting 2 strides instead of 1) due to lack of impulsion.
The rider getting ahead of the motion on the first fence and interfering with the stride.
Not enough leg between the fences, causing the horse to stop at the second element.
The horse jumping flat, not using its body correctly in the short distance.
variations: Change to a bounce (10 feet, no stride) for more technical demand.
Add a third element for a one-to-one-stride combination.
Raise the second element higher than the first for scope development.
tips: Tip: Combinations test your horse's training more than any other element. If the flatwork is correct, the combination rides itself.

title: Liverpool or Filler Introduction
steps: Place a tarp, plywood sheet, or commercial filler flat on the ground under a small cross-rail.
First, walk your horse past the tarp/filler from each side. Let it look and sniff.
When the horse is comfortable, trot over the tarp/filler on the ground without the jump.
Once confident, add the small cross-rail over the tarp/filler. Approach at the trot.
The horse may look hard at the filler. Keep your leg on firmly and your eyes up.
Repeat until the horse jumps calmly. Never punish a look — reward the effort.
mistakes: Not introducing the filler on the ground first, causing an unnecessary scare at the fence.
Hitting the horse when it looks at the filler, creating a negative association.
Approaching too fast to try to blitz the horse over the scary element.
Giving up after one refusal instead of re-approaching calmly.
variations: Use different fillers: tarps, flower boxes, barrels, decorative panels.
Place fillers under different fence types (vertical, oxer, cross-rail).
Include filler fences in a course to practice them in context.
tips: Tip: Confidence with fillers comes from positive exposure. Let the horse look, sniff, and process. Then ask it to jump. Patience wins.

title: Trot Poles to Cross-Rail
steps: Set up 3 trot poles spaced 4.5 feet apart, with a cross-rail 9 feet after the last pole.
Approach at the posting trot. Trot through the poles maintaining a steady rhythm.
After the last pole, the horse will naturally jump the cross-rail from the correct distance.
Focus on your position over the fence: heels down, eyes up, following hands.
Land and continue in a straight line at the canter for 6-8 strides.
Repeat 4-6 times. The poles create consistency in every approach.
mistakes: Picking up the canter through the trot poles and arriving too fast at the fence.
Looking down at the poles, dropping the horse onto the forehand.
Tensing through the poles in anticipation of the jump.
Not keeping enough leg through the poles, losing impulsion.
variations: Add a second fence one stride (18 feet) after the cross-rail.
Remove one trot pole at a time to gradually increase independence.
Replace the cross-rail with a small vertical to increase difficulty.
tips: Tip: Trot poles to a fence is the safest way to build jumping confidence. The poles guarantee a good distance to the fence.

title: Canter Poles to a Single Vertical
steps: Set up 3 canter poles spaced 9 feet apart, with a vertical 18 feet after the last pole (one stride).
Approach at the working canter. Canter through the poles maintaining rhythm.
After the last pole, take one stride and jump the vertical.
The canter poles establish the perfect rhythm for the approach to the fence.
Your eyes should be on the fence from the start, not on the poles.
Repeat 4-6 times. Gradually raise the vertical as the approach becomes consistent.
mistakes: Breaking rhythm through the canter poles.
Getting too close to or too far from the vertical after the one-stride.
The horse chipping in an extra stride between the last pole and the fence.
Not maintaining leg through the poles, losing impulsion before the fence.
variations: Add a second fence one stride after the first for a canter-poles-to-line exercise.
Change the one-stride to a two-stride (27 feet) for a different distance.
Remove the canter poles once the rhythm is established to test independence.
tips: Tip: Canter poles are rhythm generators. Once the rhythm is set by the poles, your job is just to maintain it to the fence.

title: Circle to a Fence
steps: Set up a single fence on the 20-meter circle line.
Establish a balanced canter on the 20-meter circle. Focus on rhythm and balance.
After 1-2 circles, jump the fence as part of the circle. Don't change anything for the fence.
The approach should feel like a continuation of the circle, not a separate event.
After the fence, continue on the circle. The canter rhythm should be the same after as before.
This exercise teaches you to jump out of rhythm rather than treating every fence as a crisis.
mistakes: Changing the canter 3-4 strides before the fence, disrupting the circle.
Cutting in toward the fence instead of maintaining the circle track.
Tightening the arms and upper body on the approach.
Landing and immediately pulling the horse back instead of continuing the circle.
variations: Add a second fence on the opposite side of the circle for a two-fence circle exercise.
Ride the circle in both directions over the fence.
Gradually raise the fence while maintaining the circle rhythm.
tips: Tip: Jumping on a circle strips away all the mental noise. There's no 'approach' — there's just a canter rhythm with a fence in the way.

title: Lead Change After a Fence
steps: Jump a small fence on the center line.
After landing, immediately plan a turn to the left (requiring left lead) or right (requiring right lead).
If you need the left lead after the fence, look left and apply your right leg behind the girth on landing.
If you get the wrong lead, trot for 3-4 strides and pick up the correct lead before the turn.
Practice getting specific leads after the fence by planning your turn before you jump.
This is essential for course riding where you need specific leads for the next line.
mistakes: Not planning the lead before the fence, then scrambling after landing.
Landing so crooked that the lead change is impossible.
Forcing the lead change with a dramatic body shift that unbalances the horse.
Always turning the same direction and never practicing the other lead.
variations: Set up two fences requiring different leads: jump fence 1, get left lead, turn to fence 2.
Practice getting both leads from the same fence by alternating turn directions.
Add a tight rollback turn after landing to practice lead and turn combination.
tips: Tip: Your eye directs the lead. Look where you're going before you leave the ground, and your body will naturally set up the correct lead.

title: Skinny Fence Practice
steps: Set up a narrow fence (4-5 feet wide instead of the standard 10-12 feet). Use cones or standards to contain the width.
Start with the skinny fence as a ground pole to establish the horse's willingness to go between the standards.
Raise to a cross-rail at 12 inches. Approach at the trot on a dead-straight line.
Your leg must be strong and equal on both sides to prevent a run-out.
Look at the center of the fence, not the edges. Your eye guides the horse's track.
Progress gradually: cross-rail to vertical. Only increase height when the horse is reliably straight.
mistakes: The horse running out to one side of the narrow fence.
Not enough supporting leg, especially the outside leg if the horse tends to drift one way.
Approaching on a curved line instead of dead straight.
Over-riding the approach and making the horse tense about the narrow fence.
variations: Place guide poles on the ground angling from the fence to funnel the horse to the center.
Jump the skinny fence from both directions to prevent side preference.
Include a skinny in a course as practice for cross-country or jump-off scenarios.
tips: Tip: Skinny fences appear in cross-country and jump-offs. Master them at low heights first. Accuracy is more important than height.

title: Bounce to Bending Line
steps: Set up a bounce (2 fences 10 feet apart), then a single fence on a bending line 60 feet from the bounce exit.
Trot into the bounce. The bounce develops quick reflexes and front leg technique.
After the bounce, canter on a bending line to the single fence in 4-5 strides.
The bending line requires you to establish a track and maintain rhythm immediately after the demanding bounce.
Practice the bending line to both left and right turns.
This combination tests both gymnastic ability and course-riding skill in one exercise.
mistakes: Losing balance in the bounce and not recovering in time for the bending line.
Not planning the bending line turn early enough — decide your direction before the bounce.
The canter after the bounce being too fast or unbalanced for the bending line.
Cutting the bending line too tight and arriving at the third fence too close.
variations: Change the bending line distance to 48 feet (3 strides direct, 4 strides bending).
Add a fourth fence after the bending line for a mini-course.
Replace the bounce with a one-stride gymnastic for a different entry.
tips: Tip: This exercise bridges the gap between gymnastic training and course riding. It teaches you to think quickly after a demanding effort.

title: Jumping Without Stirrups
steps: Cross your stirrups over the pommel of the saddle. Establish a balanced trot.
Start by trotting over a ground pole without stirrups. Focus on keeping your lower leg long and secure.
Progress to a small cross-rail (12 inches). Approach at the trot.
Without stirrups, your balance must come from your core and lower leg, not your feet.
Keep your heels down and your lower leg on the horse's side. Don't grip with your knees.
Repeat 4-6 times. Only raise the fence if your position is consistently good.
mistakes: Gripping with the knees, which actually makes you less secure.
Bouncing out of the saddle on landing because the lower leg swings back.
Grabbing the mane or the horse's mouth for balance.
Starting with fences that are too large for no-stirrup work.
variations: Progress to cantering over fences without stirrups.
Practice no-stirrup work over a gymnastic line for supported jumping.
Use this as a regular warm-up exercise (one fence, no stirrups) before every jumping session.
tips: Tip: No-stirrup jumping is the fastest way to develop an independent seat. Start small and humble — it's harder than it looks.

title: Five-Stride Line
steps: Set up 2 fences 72 feet apart (five normal canter strides).
Approach at the working canter. After the first fence, count: 1-2-3-4-5.
You should arrive at the second fence in a comfortable distance on stride 5.
If you get 6 strides, your canter needs more forward energy. If you get 4, collect slightly.
Once you can reliably get 5 strides, try 6 (collected) and 4 (open).
The ability to ride the same line in different stride counts is the mark of a skilled rider.
mistakes: Losing count and guessing at the second fence.
Pulling to add the extra stride instead of using your seat to collect.
Running to leave out a stride, arriving dangerously close to the second fence.
Not maintaining a consistent path between the fences.
variations: Practice both 4 and 6 strides in the same session for adjustability contrast.
Set up two five-stride lines at different angles for a mini-course.
Time yourself to see which stride count is fastest (hint: fewer strides, straight track).
tips: Tip: Five strides is a common show jumping distance. Know your horse's normal stride and you can add or leave out with confidence.

title: Diagonal Line with Rollback
steps: Set up a 3-stride line on the diagonal. Jump fence 1, ride 3 strides to fence 2.
After fence 2, ride a rollback turn and come back to fence 1 from the opposite direction.
The rollback should be a smooth half-circle, maintaining the canter throughout.
After jumping fence 1 again, ride the 3-stride line back to fence 2.
Continue the pattern: line, rollback, line, rollback.
This builds agility and sharpens the horse's responsiveness to turns.
mistakes: The rollback turn being too wide, wasting time and losing impulsion.
Not looking back at the fence early enough during the rollback.
The horse losing its lead during the rollback turn.
Arriving at the first fence unbalanced after the rollback.
variations: Tighten the rollback each time for a time-trial simulation.
Add a third fence offset from the line for a longer course pattern.
Change the line distance from 3 to 4 strides for a different challenge.
tips: Tip: This exercise simulates jump-off riding where tight turns and efficient lines save time.

title: Warm-Up Sequence for a Jumping Session
steps: Start with 5-10 minutes of flatwork: walk, trot, and canter in both directions with transitions.
Set up 4 trot poles and ride through them 3-4 times to establish rhythm.
Add a small cross-rail (12 inches). Trot to it 3 times from each direction.
Raise to a small vertical (18 inches). Canter to it 3 times from each direction.
If all approaches felt good, you're warmed up and ready for your main exercise.
The warm-up should take about 15-20 minutes. Never skip it.
mistakes: Skipping the flatwork warm-up and going straight to jumping.
Warming up over fences that are too large, which wears out the horse before the real work.
Rushing the warm-up because you're eager to start the main exercise.
Not establishing good flatwork rhythm before approaching the first fence.
variations: Customize your warm-up to address your horse's current weak point.
Add lateral work (shoulder-in, leg yield) to the flatwork portion for thoroughness.
Use the warm-up to practice your weakest skill before the main session.
tips: Tip: The warm-up is not optional. It prepares the horse's muscles, mind, and balance for the demands of jumping. Skip it at your own risk.

title: Cavaletti Grid at Trot
steps: Set up 6 cavaletti (or raised poles) at 4.5-foot spacing, raised 6-8 inches.
Approach at the posting trot on a straight line.
The raised height encourages the horse to round its back and engage its core muscles.
Look ahead, not down. Let the horse find the rhythm over the cavaletti.
After the cavaletti, continue trotting for 6-8 strides before turning.
Repeat 4-6 times from each direction.
mistakes: Cavaletti set too high, causing the horse to jump rather than trot over them.
Spacing incorrect for the horse's stride, causing scrambling.
The horse rushing through in anticipation.
Not approaching on a straight line.
variations: Set cavaletti on a circle for bending cavaletti work.
Alternate heights: low-high-low-high for varied engagement.
Follow cavaletti with a small fence for a gymnastic sequence.
tips: Tip: Cavaletti work builds topline muscle more effectively than any other exercise. 10 minutes of cavaletti equals 30 minutes of flat trot work for strength building.

title: Triple Combination: One-Two-One
steps: Set up 3 fences: first to second is one stride (18 feet), second to third is two strides (36 feet).
Approach at the trot. The horse will jump, take one stride, jump, take two strides, and jump the final fence.
The changing stride counts test the horse's adjustability within a combination.
Keep your eyes up and your leg active. Let the distances teach the horse.
Start with all fences as small cross-rails. Raise gradually as the horse gains confidence.
Repeat 4-6 times. The horse should smooth out with each pass.
mistakes: Approaching in canter (should be trot for consistent first distance).
Interfering with the horse's natural stride between fences.
Getting ahead of the motion on any of the three jumping efforts.
Setting the fences too high before the horse masters the distances.
variations: Reverse the distances: two strides first, then one stride.
Raise the middle fence highest for a scope exercise.
Change to bounced to one-stride (10 feet, then 18 feet) for a technical variation.
tips: Tip: Triple combinations appear in every show jumping course. Master them in training and they'll be your strength in competition.

title: Course Walk Simulation
steps: Set up a 4-6 fence course. Before riding, walk the course on foot.
Walk the track you plan to ride, counting strides between related fences.
Identify where you need to change leads, where to open the stride, and where to collect.
Look for potential problem spots: tight turns, challenging distances, unfamiliar approaches.
Walk the course at least twice. The second walk should confirm your plan.
Only mount and ride after you have a clear mental picture of every stride.
mistakes: Not walking the course at all and trying to figure it out while riding.
Walking the course once and not remembering the plan mid-ride.
Not counting strides, so distances come as surprises.
Planning an overly ambitious track instead of a safe, efficient one.
variations: Walk courses at horse shows before your round for real-world practice.
Have someone quiz you on the course after walking it to test your memory.
Walk the course backward to see it from a different perspective.
tips: Tip: Professional show jumpers walk every course twice. If it's good enough for them, it's good enough for you. Never skip the course walk.

title: Jumping Position at the Walk and Canter
steps: At the walk, rise into two-point position: your seat lifts slightly off the saddle, weight drops into your heels.
Hold the two-point position for 10 strides, then sit for 10 strides. Alternate.
Your hands should stay steady over the horse's withers. Don't brace on the neck.
Progress to the walk in two-point. This is harder because of the rocking motion.
Hold two-point at the walk for 10 strides, then sit. Build up to 20, then 30 strides.
This develops the leg strength and balance needed for jumping.
mistakes: Gripping with the knees instead of keeping weight in the heels.
Resting hands on the horse's neck for balance instead of being self-supporting.
Collapsing the upper body forward instead of maintaining a flat back with eyes up.
Holding breath — keep breathing normally throughout.
variations: Hold two-point for an entire trot circle (30+ strides) for endurance.
Practice two-point while trotting over ground poles.
Add arm exercises in two-point: arms out to the side, arms forward, hands on helmet.
tips: Tip: Two-point is the foundation of jumping position. If you can't hold it for 30 seconds at the canter, you're not ready for courses.

title: Jumping Position at the Trot and Canter at the Trot
steps: At the trot, rise into two-point position: your seat lifts slightly off the saddle, weight drops into your heels.
Hold the two-point position for 10 strides, then sit for 10 strides. Alternate.
Your hands should stay steady over the horse's withers. Don't brace on the neck.
Progress to the trot in two-point. This is harder because of the rocking motion.
Hold two-point at the trot for 10 strides, then sit. Build up to 20, then 30 strides.
This develops the leg strength and balance needed for jumping.
mistakes: Gripping with the knees instead of keeping weight in the heels.
Resting hands on the horse's neck for balance instead of being self-supporting.
Collapsing the upper body forward instead of maintaining a flat back with eyes up.
Holding breath — keep breathing normally throughout.
variations: Hold two-point for an entire trot circle (30+ strides) for endurance.
Practice two-point while trotting over ground poles.
Add arm exercises in two-point: arms out to the side, arms forward, hands on helmet.
tips: Tip: Two-point is the foundation of jumping position. If you can't hold it for 30 seconds at the canter, you're not ready for courses.

title: Jumping Position at the Canter and Canter
steps: At the canter, rise into two-point position: your seat lifts slightly off the saddle, weight drops into your heels.
Hold the two-point position for 10 strides, then sit for 10 strides. Alternate.
Your hands should stay steady over the horse's withers. Don't brace on the neck.
Progress to the canter in two-point. This is harder because of the rocking motion.
Hold two-point at the canter for 10 strides, then sit. Build up to 20, then 30 strides.
This develops the leg strength and balance needed for jumping.
mistakes: Gripping with the knees instead of keeping weight in the heels.
Resting hands on the horse's neck for balance instead of being self-supporting.
Collapsing the upper body forward instead of maintaining a flat back with eyes up.
Holding breath — keep breathing normally throughout.
variations: Hold two-point for an entire trot circle (30+ strides) for endurance.
Practice two-point while trotting over ground poles.
Add arm exercises in two-point: arms out to the side, arms forward, hands on helmet.
tips: Tip: Two-point is the foundation of jumping position. If you can't hold it for 30 seconds at the canter, you're not ready for courses.

title: Jumping Position at the Sitting Trot and Canter
steps: At the sitting trot, rise into two-point position: your seat lifts slightly off the saddle, weight drops into your heels.
Hold the two-point position for 10 strides, then sit for 10 strides. Alternate.
Your hands should stay steady over the horse's withers. Don't brace on the neck.
Progress to the sitting trot in two-point. This is harder because of the rocking motion.
Hold two-point at the sitting trot for 10 strides, then sit. Build up to 20, then 30 strides.
This develops the leg strength and balance needed for jumping.
mistakes: Gripping with the knees instead of keeping weight in the heels.
Resting hands on the horse's neck for balance instead of being self-supporting.
Collapsing the upper body forward instead of maintaining a flat back with eyes up.
Holding breath — keep breathing normally throughout.
variations: Hold two-point for an entire trot circle (30+ strides) for endurance.
Practice two-point while trotting over ground poles.
Add arm exercises in two-point: arms out to the side, arms forward, hands on helmet.
tips: Tip: Two-point is the foundation of jumping position. If you can't hold it for 30 seconds at the canter, you're not ready for courses.

title: Jumping Position at the Collected Trot and Canter
steps: At the collected trot, rise into two-point position: your seat lifts slightly off the saddle, weight drops into your heels.
Hold the two-point position for 10 strides, then sit for 10 strides. Alternate.
Your hands should stay steady over the horse's withers. Don't brace on the neck.
Progress to the collected trot in two-point. This is harder because of the rocking motion.
Hold two-point at the collected trot for 10 strides, then sit. Build up to 20, then 30 strides.
This develops the leg strength and balance needed for jumping.
mistakes: Gripping with the knees instead of keeping weight in the heels.
Resting hands on the horse's neck for balance instead of being self-supporting.
Collapsing the upper body forward instead of maintaining a flat back with eyes up.
Holding breath — keep breathing normally throughout.
variations: Hold two-point for an entire trot circle (30+ strides) for endurance.
Practice two-point while trotting over ground poles.
Add arm exercises in two-point: arms out to the side, arms forward, hands on helmet.
tips: Tip: Two-point is the foundation of jumping position. If you can't hold it for 30 seconds at the canter, you're not ready for courses.

title: Placing Pole to a Single Fence at the Walk
steps: Set up a small vertical with a placing pole 9 feet in front of it (one walk stride).
Approach at the walk. The placing pole ensures you arrive at the correct takeoff distance every time.
Walk over the placing pole. The horse will naturally take one stride and jump the fence.
Focus on your position: heels down, eyes up, following hands, soft seat.
The consistency of the placing pole allows you to work on your form without worrying about the distance.
Repeat 6-8 times, gradually raising the fence as confidence grows.
mistakes: Approaching in canter instead of trot, which changes the placing pole distance.
Looking down at the placing pole instead of at the fence.
Tensing up and grabbing the mane over the fence.
Not keeping enough leg on the approach, causing the horse to stop.
variations: Move the placing pole to 18 feet for a canter approach with one stride.
Remove the placing pole after several successful attempts to test your independent judgment.
Add a second fence one stride after the first for a gymnastic sequence.
tips: Tip: Placing poles are training wheels for distance. Use them liberally, then gradually remove them as your eye develops.

title: Placing Pole to a Single Fence at the Trot
steps: Set up a small vertical with a placing pole 9 feet in front of it (one trot stride).
Approach at the trot. The placing pole ensures you arrive at the correct takeoff distance every time.
Trot over the placing pole. The horse will naturally take one stride and jump the fence.
Focus on your position: heels down, eyes up, following hands, soft seat.
The consistency of the placing pole allows you to work on your form without worrying about the distance.
Repeat 6-8 times, gradually raising the fence as confidence grows.
mistakes: Approaching in canter instead of trot, which changes the placing pole distance.
Looking down at the placing pole instead of at the fence.
Tensing up and grabbing the mane over the fence.
Not keeping enough leg on the approach, causing the horse to stop.
variations: Move the placing pole to 18 feet for a canter approach with one stride.
Remove the placing pole after several successful attempts to test your independent judgment.
Add a second fence one stride after the first for a gymnastic sequence.
tips: Tip: Placing poles are training wheels for distance. Use them liberally, then gradually remove them as your eye develops.

title: Placing Pole to a Single Fence at the Canter
steps: Set up a small vertical with a placing pole 9 feet in front of it (one canter stride).
Approach at the canter. The placing pole ensures you arrive at the correct takeoff distance every time.
Canter over the placing pole. The horse will naturally take one stride and jump the fence.
Focus on your position: heels down, eyes up, following hands, soft seat.
The consistency of the placing pole allows you to work on your form without worrying about the distance.
Repeat 6-8 times, gradually raising the fence as confidence grows.
mistakes: Approaching in canter instead of trot, which changes the placing pole distance.
Looking down at the placing pole instead of at the fence.
Tensing up and grabbing the mane over the fence.
Not keeping enough leg on the approach, causing the horse to stop.
variations: Move the placing pole to 18 feet for a canter approach with one stride.
Remove the placing pole after several successful attempts to test your independent judgment.
Add a second fence one stride after the first for a gymnastic sequence.
tips: Tip: Placing poles are training wheels for distance. Use them liberally, then gradually remove them as your eye develops.

title: Placing Pole to a Single Fence at the Sitting Trot
steps: Set up a small vertical with a placing pole 9 feet in front of it (one sitting trot stride).
Approach at the sitting trot. The placing pole ensures you arrive at the correct takeoff distance every time.
Sitting Trot over the placing pole. The horse will naturally take one stride and jump the fence.
Focus on your position: heels down, eyes up, following hands, soft seat.
The consistency of the placing pole allows you to work on your form without worrying about the distance.
Repeat 6-8 times, gradually raising the fence as confidence grows.
mistakes: Approaching in canter instead of trot, which changes the placing pole distance.
Looking down at the placing pole instead of at the fence.
Tensing up and grabbing the mane over the fence.
Not keeping enough leg on the approach, causing the horse to stop.
variations: Move the placing pole to 18 feet for a canter approach with one stride.
Remove the placing pole after several successful attempts to test your independent judgment.
Add a second fence one stride after the first for a gymnastic sequence.
tips: Tip: Placing poles are training wheels for distance. Use them liberally, then gradually remove them as your eye develops.

title: Placing Pole to a Single Fence at the Collected Trot
steps: Set up a small vertical with a placing pole 9 feet in front of it (one collected trot stride).
Approach at the collected trot. The placing pole ensures you arrive at the correct takeoff distance every time.
Collected Trot over the placing pole. The horse will naturally take one stride and jump the fence.
Focus on your position: heels down, eyes up, following hands, soft seat.
The consistency of the placing pole allows you to work on your form without worrying about the distance.
Repeat 6-8 times, gradually raising the fence as confidence grows.
mistakes: Approaching in canter instead of trot, which changes the placing pole distance.
Looking down at the placing pole instead of at the fence.
Tensing up and grabbing the mane over the fence.
Not keeping enough leg on the approach, causing the horse to stop.
variations: Move the placing pole to 18 feet for a canter approach with one stride.
Remove the placing pole after several successful attempts to test your independent judgment.
Add a second fence one stride after the first for a gymnastic sequence.
tips: Tip: Placing poles are training wheels for distance. Use them liberally, then gradually remove them as your eye develops.

title: One-Stride Gymnastic at the Walk
steps: Set up a placing pole, then a cross-rail 9 feet later, then a second cross-rail 18 feet after the first (one stride).
Approach at the walk. The placing pole helps the horse arrive at the correct takeoff distance.
The horse jumps the first cross-rail, lands, takes one walk stride, and jumps the second.
Your job is to maintain balance and rhythm. Don't interfere with the horse's natural arc.
Keep your eyes up and your leg on. Allow the horse to figure out the striding.
Start low (12 inches) and gradually raise to 18 inches as the horse gains confidence.
mistakes: Looking down at the fences instead of up and ahead.
Pulling on the reins between the two fences, shortening the horse's stride.
Throwing the upper body forward over each jump, getting ahead of the motion.
Approaching in canter instead of trot, which changes the distance.
variations: Add a third fence at 18 feet for a two-stride gymnastic.
Change the distances: 17 feet for a shorter stride, 19 feet for a longer stride.
Raise to verticals at 2-2.5 feet for more challenge.
tips: Tip: Gymnastics teach your horse to jump correctly without you having to micromanage the takeoff. Trust the exercise.

title: One-Stride Pole Exercise at the Walk
steps: Set up 2 ground poles spaced 18 feet apart (one walk stride between them).
Approach at the walk. The horse should land after the first pole, take one walk stride, and step over the second pole.
Focus on maintaining a steady walk rhythm. Don't adjust the horse's stride — let the poles teach.
If the horse chips in an extra stride, the walk is too slow. Approach with more impulsion next time.
If the horse gets too close to the second pole, the walk is too fast. Half-halt on approach.
Repeat 4-6 times from each direction until the one-stride feels effortless.
mistakes: Adding an extra stride because the canter lacks impulsion.
The horse jumping both poles in one big leap instead of striding between them.
The rider interfering by pulling or pushing at the wrong moment.
Not approaching on a straight line, causing the horse to drift sideways.
variations: Change the distance to 21 feet (one longer stride) to practice lengthening.
Add a third pole at 18 feet for a two-stride exercise.
Replace the ground poles with small cross-rails for a one-stride jumping exercise.
tips: Tip: Pole distances teach your eye. Once you can see a one-stride from 6 strides away, you're developing the feel for related distances.

title: Inside Turn Between Two Fences at the Walk
steps: Set up two fences facing the same direction, about 40 feet apart laterally.
Jump the first fence, then ride an inside turn (toward the center of the arena) to approach the second fence.
The inside turn saves time compared to going wide around the end of the arena.
You must land, look immediately to the inside, half-halt, and bend the horse toward the second fence.
Start with a wider inside turn and gradually tighten as you and your horse gain confidence.
Practice turning to both the left and right for even development.
mistakes: Starting the turn too late, running past the second fence.
The turn being so tight that the horse loses balance and chips in extra strides.
Not looking at the second fence soon enough — your eye directs the turn.
Landing on the wrong lead, making the inside turn difficult.
variations: Set up 4 fences in a box and practice inside turns between each pair.
Alternate inside and outside turns to the same fences.
Time yourself to see how much faster the inside turn is versus going wide.
tips: Tip: Inside turns are all about your eye. Look at the second fence before you leave the ground over the first. Your body will follow your eyes.

title: Angled Approach to a Fence at the Walk
steps: Set up a single vertical. Place cones to mark an approach line that angles across the fence (not perpendicular).
The angle should be mild — about 15-20 degrees off straight.
Approach on the angled line at the walk. Keep the horse straight on YOUR line, not the fence's line.
The horse should jump at an angle, landing on the same angled track on the other side.
Your outside rein and leg keep the horse from drifting to jump the fence straight.
Start with a small fence and mild angle. Gradually increase the angle as confidence builds.
mistakes: The horse drifting to jump the fence straight instead of on the angled path.
Not enough supporting outside aids, allowing the horse to run out or drift.
The approach becoming crooked — the horse should be straight on your line.
Jumping the fence at too sharp an angle, risking a run-out or stop.
variations: Practice angled approaches from both sides of the fence.
Set up two fences and practice an angled approach to one that leads to a straight approach on the other.
Use angle fences as part of a course for jump-off practice.
tips: Tip: Angled fences appear in every jump-off. Master them by starting with a small angle and building up gradually.

title: Cavaletti Grid at Walk
steps: Set up 6 cavaletti (or raised poles) at 4.5-foot spacing, raised 6-8 inches.
Approach at the posting walk on a straight line.
The raised height encourages the horse to round its back and engage its core muscles.
Look ahead, not down. Let the horse find the rhythm over the cavaletti.
After the cavaletti, continue walkting for 6-8 strides before turning.
Repeat 4-6 times from each direction.
mistakes: Cavaletti set too high, causing the horse to jump rather than trot over them.
Spacing incorrect for the horse's stride, causing scrambling.
The horse rushing through in anticipation.
Not approaching on a straight line.
variations: Set cavaletti on a circle for bending cavaletti work.
Alternate heights: low-high-low-high for varied engagement.
Follow cavaletti with a small fence for a gymnastic sequence.
tips: Tip: Cavaletti work builds topline muscle more effectively than any other exercise. 10 minutes of cavaletti equals 30 minutes of flat trot work for strength building.
