title: Walk Poles to a Single Vertical
steps: Set up 3 walk poles spaced 9 feet apart, with a vertical 18 feet after the last pole (one stride).
Approach at the working walk. Walk through the poles maintaining rhythm.
After the last pole, take one stride and jump the vertical.
The walk poles establish the perfect rhythm for the approach to the fence.
Your eyes should be on the fence from the start, not on the poles.
Repeat 4-6 times. Gradually raise the vertical as the approach becomes consistent.
mistakes: Breaking rhythm through the canter poles.
Getting too close to or too far from the vertical after the one-stride.
The horse chipping in an extra stride between the last pole and the fence.
Not maintaining leg through the poles, losing impulsion before the fence.
variations: Add a second fence one stride after the first for a canter-poles-to-line exercise.
Change the one-stride to a two-stride (27 feet) for a different distance.
Remove the canter poles once the rhythm is established to test independence.
tips: Tip: Canter poles are rhythm generators. Once the rhythm is set by the poles, your job is just to maintain it to the fence.

title: Trot Poles to a Single Vertical
steps: Set up 3 trot poles spaced 9 feet apart, with a vertical 18 feet after the last pole (one stride).

After the last pole, take one stride and jump the vertical.
The trot poles establish the perfect rhythm for the approach to the fence.
Your eyes should be on the fence from the start, not on the poles.
Repeat 4-6 times. Gradually raise the vertical as the approach becomes consistent.
mistakes: Breaking rhythm through the canter poles.
Getting too close to or too far from the vertical after the one-stride.
The horse chipping in an extra stride between the last pole and the fence.

Remove the canter poles once the rhythm is established to test independence.
tips: Tip: Canter poles are rhythm generators. Once the rhythm is set by the poles, your job is just to maintain it to the fence.

title: Canter Poles to a Single Vertical at the Canter
steps: Set up 3 canter poles spaced 9 feet apart, with a vertical 18 feet after the last pole (one stride).
Approach at the working canter. Canter through the poles maintaining rhythm.
After the last pole, take one stride and jump the vertical.
The canter poles establish the perfect rhythm for the approach to the fence.
Your eyes should be on the fence from the start, not on the poles.
Repeat 4-6 times. Gradually raise the vertical as the approach becomes consistent.
mistakes: Breaking rhythm through the canter poles.
Getting too close to or too far from the vertical after the one-stride.
The horse chipping in an extra stride between the last pole and the fence.
Not maintaining leg through the poles, losing impulsion before the fence.
variations: Add a second fence one stride after the first for a canter-poles-to-line exercise.
Change the one-stride to a two-stride (27 feet) for a different distance.
Remove the canter poles once the rhythm is established to test independence.
tips: Tip: Canter poles are rhythm generators. Once the rhythm is set by the poles, your job is just to maintain it to the fence.

title: Ground Pole Grid: Four-Stride Line at the Walk
steps: Set up 2 ground poles with 60 feet between them (4 normal walk strides).
Place cones alongside to mark a straight track.
Approach at the working walk. Count your strides: 1-2-3-4. The horse should arrive at the second pole in exactly 4 strides.
If you get 5 strides, open your horse's stride next time. If you get 3, collect more.
Now try to fit 5 strides in the same distance (shorter, more collected strides).
Then try 3 strides (longer, more open strides).
This adjustability is the foundation of all course riding.
mistakes: Counting strides wrong — start counting when the horse lands after the first pole.
Pulling on the reins to add strides instead of using your seat and half-halts.
Kicking to remove strides instead of opening your body and allowing a bigger canter.
Not approaching on a straight line, making the distance longer or shorter than intended.
variations: Set up two different four-stride lines and ride them on a course.
Add cross-rails instead of ground poles for a more realistic exercise.
Practice the exercise counting down: 4-3-2-1 to develop awareness of where each stride falls.
tips: Tip: Four strides is the most common related distance in show jumping. Master this and you can ride any course.

title: Bounce to One-Stride Combination at the Walk
steps: Set up: placing pole, then cross-rail, then 10 feet to a second cross-rail (bounce), then 18 feet to a vertical (one stride).
Approach at the walk. The placing pole sets up the first jump.
Bounce the first two fences, then the horse has one stride to the third fence.
The bounce develops quickness; the one-stride develops scope and bascule.
Repeat 4-6 times, gradually raising the fences as the horse gains confidence.
If the horse struggles, lower everything and rebuild confidence.
mistakes: The horse rushing after the bounce, arriving too close to the third fence.
Not enough impulsion to clear the third fence after the demanding bounce.
The rider getting ahead of the motion in the bounce and interfering with the one-stride.
Setting the fences too high before the horse is ready for the combination.
variations: Extend to bounce-one stride-one stride for a longer exercise.
Add an oxer as the final fence for scope development.
Change the one-stride distance to test adjustability: 17 feet vs. 19 feet.
tips: Tip: This is a classic gymnastic combination. The bounce teaches quick feet; the one-stride teaches the horse to push and stretch.

title: Diagonal Line with Rollback at the Walk
steps: Set up a 3-stride line on the diagonal. Jump fence 1, ride 3 strides to fence 2.
After fence 2, ride a rollback turn and come back to fence 1 from the opposite direction.
The rollback should be a smooth half-circle, maintaining the walk throughout.
After jumping fence 1 again, ride the 3-stride line back to fence 2.
Continue the pattern: line, rollback, line, rollback.
This builds agility and sharpens the horse's responsiveness to turns.
mistakes: The rollback turn being too wide, wasting time and losing impulsion.
Not looking back at the fence early enough during the rollback.
The horse losing its lead during the rollback turn.
Arriving at the first fence unbalanced after the rollback.
variations: Tighten the rollback each time for a time-trial simulation.
Add a third fence offset from the line for a longer course pattern.
Change the line distance from 3 to 4 strides for a different challenge.
tips: Tip: This exercise simulates jump-off riding where tight turns and efficient lines save time.

title: Course Walk Simulation at the Walk
steps: Set up a 4-6 fence course. Before riding, walk the course on foot.
Walk the track you plan to ride, counting strides between related fences.
Identify where you need to change leads, where to open the stride, and where to collect.
Look for potential problem spots: tight turns, challenging distances, unfamiliar approaches.
Walk the course at least twice. The second walk should confirm your plan.
Only mount and ride after you have a clear mental picture of every stride.
mistakes: Not walking the course at all and trying to figure it out while riding.
Walking the course once and not remembering the plan mid-ride.
Not counting strides, so distances come as surprises.
Planning an overly ambitious track instead of a safe, efficient one.
variations: Walk courses at horse shows before your round for real-world practice.
Have someone quiz you on the course after walking it to test your memory.
Walk the course backward to see it from a different perspective.
tips: Tip: Professional show jumpers walk every course twice. If it's good enough for them, it's good enough for you. Never skip the course walk.

title: Walk Poles in a Straight Line
steps: Set up 4 ground poles in a straight line, spaced 4.5 feet apart (one normal walk stride).
Approach at the posting walk on a straight line. Look ahead past the poles, not down at them.
Allow the horse to find its rhythm over the poles. Keep a steady contact but don't interfere.
The horse should walk through without speeding up, slowing down, or jumping the poles.
After the poles, continue walkting in a straight line for 6-8 strides before turning.
Repeat 4-6 times from each direction. The horse should become more confident with each pass.
mistakes: Looking down at the poles instead of ahead, which drops the horse onto the forehand.
Pulling on the reins as the horse approaches, causing it to shorten its stride and hit the poles.
Letting the horse rush through the poles — maintain the same trot tempo before, during, and after.
Leaning forward over the poles, getting ahead of the horse's motion.
variations: Increase to 6 poles for a longer exercise.
Raise alternate poles on one end (1-2 inches) to encourage the horse to lift its legs higher.
Fan the poles on a curve to practice trot poles on a bending line.
tips: Tip: Let the horse figure out the poles. Your job is to maintain rhythm and direction. The horse will learn to adjust its stride.

title: Collected Trot Poles in a Straight Line
steps: Set up 4 ground poles in a straight line, spaced 4.5 feet apart (one normal collected trot stride).
Approach at the posting collected trot on a straight line. Look ahead past the poles, not down at them.
Allow the horse to find its rhythm over the poles. Keep a steady contact but don't interfere.
The horse should collected trot through without speeding up, slowing down, or jumping the poles.
After the poles, continue collected trotting in a straight line for 6-8 strides before turning.
Repeat 4-6 times from each direction. The horse should become more confident with each pass.
mistakes: Looking down at the poles instead of ahead, which drops the horse onto the forehand.
Pulling on the reins as the horse approaches, causing it to shorten its stride and hit the poles.
Letting the horse rush through the poles — maintain the same trot tempo before, during, and after.
Leaning forward over the poles, getting ahead of the horse's motion.
variations: Increase to 6 poles for a longer exercise.
Raise alternate poles on one end (1-2 inches) to encourage the horse to lift its legs higher.
Fan the poles on a curve to practice trot poles on a bending line.
tips: Tip: Let the horse figure out the poles. Your job is to maintain rhythm and direction. The horse will learn to adjust its stride.

title: Walk-Halt-Walk on a Straight Line
steps: Set up 4 cones in a line, 60 feet apart, along the long side.
Walk from cone 1 to cone 2. At cone 2, halt. Stand for 3 seconds.
From the halt, immediately pick up walk to cone 3. Halt at cone 3.
Continue the walk-halt-walk pattern through all 4 cones.
The halts should be prompt and square. The walk departures should be clean and on the correct lead.
This exercise develops the responsiveness needed for adjustability in jumping courses.
mistakes: Trotting between the halt and canter instead of making clean transitions.
The halts being messy — the horse walking forward or swinging sideways.
Losing the correct lead on the canter departures.
The canter becoming tense and rushed from the repeated halts.
variations: Reduce to 3 cones for a simpler version.
Replace halts with walk transitions (3 walk steps) for an easier version.
Add this exercise before a gymnastics session to sharpen the horse's responsiveness.
tips: Tip: If your horse can halt cleanly from canter and depart cleanly from halt, it has the obedience to handle any distance question in a course.

title: Walk Poles in a Line
steps: Set up 4 ground poles spaced 9 feet apart (one normal walk stride between each).
Approach at the working walk. Establish a steady rhythm 6-8 strides before the poles.
Look ahead past the poles. Keep your leg on to maintain impulsion through the poles.
The horse should walk through with one stride between each pole without chipping in extra strides.
After the poles, maintain the walk for 6-8 strides before transitioning or turning.
Repeat from both leads. Note if the horse is more comfortable on one lead.
mistakes: The horse breaking to trot before or during the poles.
Chipping in an extra stride between poles because the canter is too short.
The horse jumping the poles instead of cantering over them.
The rider throwing their upper body forward at each pole.
variations: Increase the spacing to 10 feet to encourage a longer stride.
Decrease to 8 feet to develop collection and adjustability.
Raise the poles to 6 inches to create low cavaletti work.
tips: Tip: If the horse adds strides, the canter is too slow. If it leaves out strides, the canter is too fast. Adjust the canter, not the pole spacing.

title: Rollback Turn to a Single Fence at the Walk
steps: Set up a single vertical in the center of the arena.
Jump the fence, then immediately ride a tight rollback turn (half-circle) and jump it again from the other direction.
The rollback should be a smooth half-circle, not a sharp pivot. Maintain the walk throughout.
Start with a wide rollback (20-meter half-circle) and gradually tighten to 10-15 meters.
The horse needs to land, rebalance on the turn, and set up for the next approach in just a few strides.
Practice rollbacks to both directions for even development.
mistakes: The turn being too tight, causing the horse to break to trot or lose balance.
Not looking early enough in the direction of the rollback turn.
Landing and running straight for too many strides before starting the turn.
The second approach being rushed and unbalanced.
variations: Set up two fences and alternate rollback turns between them.
Tighten the rollback to simulate jump-off turns.
Add a related distance after the rollback: rollback to two-stride line.
tips: Tip: Rollbacks win jump-offs. The tighter your rollback, the fewer strides you waste. But tightness should never sacrifice balance.

title: Three-Stride Related Distance at the Walk
steps: Set up 2 small fences 48 feet apart (three normal walk strides).
Approach at the working walk. After the first fence, count your strides: 1-2-3.
You should arrive at the second fence with a comfortable distance on stride 3.
Keep your leg on and your eyes up. Let the walk rhythm carry you to the fence.
If you get 4 strides, your walk is too slow. If you get 2, it's too fast.
Repeat 4-6 times until the 3-stride line feels automatic.
mistakes: Counting strides wrong — start counting when the horse's front feet land after the first fence.
Changing the canter speed between the fences instead of maintaining rhythm.
Looking down between the fences instead of at the second fence.
Tensing up after the first fence because you're worried about the distance.
variations: Try the 3-stride in 4 strides (collected) or 2 strides (open) for adjustability.
Add a third fence for a 3-stride to 3-stride exercise.
Approach from trot for a different first-fence dynamic.
tips: Tip: Three strides gives you plenty of time to adjust. If you maintain rhythm, the distance takes care of itself.

Approach at the working trot. After the first fence, count your strides: 1-2-3.

Keep your leg on and your eyes up. Let the trot rhythm carry you to the fence.
If you get 4 strides, your trot is too slow. If you get 2, it's too fast.
Repeat 4-6 times until the 3-stride line feels automatic.

title: Two-Stride Related Distance at the Walk
steps: Set up 2 fences 36 feet apart (two normal walk strides).
Approach at the working walk. After the first fence, the horse should land and take exactly 2 strides to the second.
Keep your leg on between the fences. Look up at the second fence, not down.
If the horse adds a third stride, approach with more impulsion next time.
If the horse gets to the second fence in one big stride, collect on the approach.
Practice until 2 strides feels automatic.
mistakes: Panicking between the fences and pulling the horse back.
Not enough impulsion, causing a chip-in third stride.
The horse jumping flat over the first fence, landing too far into the line.
The rider interfering with the horse's natural stride between fences.
variations: Try 35 feet for a steady 2 strides and 37 feet for a forward 2 strides.
Add a third fence at 36 feet for a 2-stride to 2-stride combination.
Ride the 2-stride in 3 strides (collected) for advanced adjustability.
tips: Tip: Two strides is the most common combination distance. If you can ride it cleanly every time, you can handle anything the course designer throws at you.

title: Three-Element Bounce Grid at the Walk
steps: Set up 3 cross-rails, each 10 feet apart (bounce-bounce).
Approach at the walk. The horse bounces through all three fences without a stride between any of them.
This is physically demanding. Start at very low heights (12 inches) and only raise when the horse is comfortable.
Keep your upper body centered and your leg secure. Your hands must follow the horse's mouth over each fence.
The horse will develop explosive hind leg power and quick front leg folding from this exercise.
Limit to 4-5 passes per session. Bounces are hard work.
mistakes: The horse losing momentum and stopping at the second or third element.
Setting the fences too high for a three-bounce, causing the horse to struggle.
The rider getting bounced out of the saddle because of the rapid succession.
The horse drifting sideways through the grid.
variations: Progress to a four-element bounce for advanced horses.
Mix cross-rails and verticals: cross-rail, vertical, cross-rail.
Add a one-stride after the final bounce to a slightly larger fence.
tips: Tip: Three bounces is the sweet spot — enough repetition to build technique without being so long that the horse loses energy.

title: Three-Element Bounce Grid at the Trot
steps: Set up 3 cross-rails, each 10 feet apart (bounce-bounce).
Approach at the trot. The horse bounces through all three fences without a stride between any of them.
This is physically demanding. Start at very low heights (12 inches) and only raise when the horse is comfortable.
Keep your upper body centered and your leg secure. Your hands must follow the horse's mouth over each fence.
The horse will develop explosive hind leg power and quick front leg folding from this exercise.
Limit to 4-5 passes per session. Bounces are hard work.
mistakes: The horse losing momentum and stopping at the second or third element.
Setting the fences too high for a three-bounce, causing the horse to struggle.
The rider getting bounced out of the saddle because of the rapid succession.
The horse drifting sideways through the grid.
variations: Progress to a four-element bounce for advanced horses.
Mix cross-rails and verticals: cross-rail, vertical, cross-rail.
Add a one-stride after the final bounce to a slightly larger fence.
tips: Tip: Three bounces is the sweet spot — enough repetition to build technique without being so long that the horse loses energy.

title: Gymnastic Line: Walk In, Canter Out
steps: Set up: walk pole at 8 feet, cross-rail, 18 feet to a vertical, 19 feet to a slightly larger vertical.
Approach at the walk. The walk pole ensures a correct takeoff to the first fence.
The horse will walk after the first fence. Use the one-stride distances to maintain rhythm.
Each fence can be slightly larger than the previous one, creating a building exercise.
Keep your eyes up and your leg active. Let the gymnastic do the training.
The final fence can be raised 3-6 inches higher than the others to develop scope.
mistakes: Approaching in canter instead of trot, which throws off the first distance.
Interfering with the horse's canter between fences.
Getting ahead of the motion and weighing down the forehand.
Raising the fences too quickly before the horse builds confidence.
variations: Add a fourth element at one stride to extend the gymnastic.
Replace the final fence with an oxer for scope development.
Add a bending line after the gymnastic to a single fence for course simulation.
tips: Tip: Gymnastics are the most efficient way to improve a horse's jumping technique. 15 minutes of grid work equals an hour of course practice.

title: Gallop Position and Pace Control at the Walk
steps: In a large arena or field, establish a walk. Rise into gallop position (short stirrups, upper body forward, weight in heels).
Gallop for a set distance (between two cones), then collect to a working walk.
The transition from gallop to working walk should be smooth: close your hip angle, sit deeper, half-halt.
Practice opening and closing the gallop 3-4 times down the long side.
This simulates the pace control needed on cross-country courses.
Always maintain control. A controlled gallop is a gear you choose, not a speed you can't stop.
mistakes: Letting the gallop get away from you — always be able to stop within 6 strides.
Pulling on the horse's mouth to slow down instead of using your body.
Rising too far out of the saddle, losing lower leg contact.
Not practicing the collect-back portion enough — it's the harder skill.
variations: Set up cones at specific points for gallop-to-canter transitions.
Practice galloping to a single fence for cross-country simulation.
Time yourself between two points at gallop and working canter to feel the speed difference.
tips: Tip: A good gallop feels fast but controlled. If you can't collect at any moment, you're going too fast.

title: Walk Poles to Cross-Rail
steps: Set up 3 walk poles spaced 4.5 feet apart, with a cross-rail 9 feet after the last pole.
Approach at the posting walk. Walk through the poles maintaining a steady rhythm.
After the last pole, the horse will naturally jump the cross-rail from the correct distance.
Focus on your position over the fence: heels down, eyes up, following hands.
Land and continue in a straight line at the walk for 6-8 strides.
Repeat 4-6 times. The poles create consistency in every approach.
mistakes: Picking up the canter through the trot poles and arriving too fast at the fence.
Looking down at the poles, dropping the horse onto the forehand.
Tensing through the poles in anticipation of the jump.
Not keeping enough leg through the poles, losing impulsion.
variations: Add a second fence one stride (18 feet) after the cross-rail.
Remove one trot pole at a time to gradually increase independence.
Replace the cross-rail with a small vertical to increase difficulty.
tips: Tip: Trot poles to a fence is the safest way to build jumping confidence. The poles guarantee a good distance to the fence.

title: Walk Adjustability Between Poles
steps: Set up 2 ground poles 72 feet apart (approximately 5 normal walk strides).
Approach at the working walk. Count your strides between the poles: you should get 5.
On the next pass, try to fit 6 strides (collected, shorter walk).
On the third pass, try to fit 4 strides (open, longer walk without running).
The goal is to ride the same distance in different stride counts by adjusting stride length.
Practice until you can reliably hit 4, 5, or 6 strides on demand.
mistakes: Changing the tempo (speed) instead of the stride length to adjust the count.
Not committing to the adjustment — half-hearted collection that doesn't change the count.
Pulling on the reins to add strides instead of using seat and half-halts.
Running at the poles to leave out strides, arriving dangerously close to the second pole.
variations: Change the distance to 60 feet for a 4-stride normal, 3/5 stride adjustability exercise.
Add a third pole for two consecutive distance questions.
Replace poles with small fences for real jumping adjustability.
tips: Tip: Stride adjustability is the single most important skill in jumping. A rider who can add or leave out a stride can ride any course.

title: Liverpool or Filler Introduction at the Walk
steps: Place a tarp, plywood sheet, or commercial filler flat on the ground under a small cross-rail.
First, walk your horse past the tarp/filler from each side. Let it look and sniff.
When the horse is comfortable, walk over the tarp/filler on the ground without the jump.
Once confident, add the small cross-rail over the tarp/filler. Approach at the walk.
The horse may look hard at the filler. Keep your leg on firmly and your eyes up.
Repeat until the horse jumps calmly. Never punish a look — reward the effort.
mistakes: Not introducing the filler on the ground first, causing an unnecessary scare at the fence.
Hitting the horse when it looks at the filler, creating a negative association.
Approaching too fast to try to blitz the horse over the scary element.
Giving up after one refusal instead of re-approaching calmly.
variations: Use different fillers: tarps, flower boxes, barrels, decorative panels.
Place fillers under different fence types (vertical, oxer, cross-rail).
Include filler fences in a course to practice them in context.
tips: Tip: Confidence with fillers comes from positive exposure. Let the horse look, sniff, and process. Then ask it to jump. Patience wins.

tips: Tip: Confidence with fillers comes from positive exposure. Let the horse look, sniff, and process. Then ask it to jump. Patience wins.

title: Liverpool or Filler Introduction at the Sitting Trot
steps: Place a tarp, plywood sheet, or commercial filler flat on the ground under a small cross-rail.
First, sitting sitting trot your horse past the tarp/filler from each side. Let it look and sniff.
When the horse is comfortable, sitting trot over the tarp/filler on the ground without the jump.
Once confident, add the small cross-rail over the tarp/filler. Approach at the sitting trot.
The horse may look hard at the filler. Keep your leg on firmly and your eyes up.
Repeat until the horse jumps calmly. Never punish a look — reward the effort.
mistakes: Not introducing the filler on the ground first, causing an unnecessary scare at the fence.
Hitting the horse when it looks at the filler, creating a negative association.
Approaching too fast to try to blitz the horse over the scary element.
Giving up after one refusal instead of re-approaching calmly.
variations: Use different fillers: tarps, flower boxes, barrels, decorative panels.
Place fillers under different fence types (vertical, oxer, cross-rail).
Include filler fences in a course to practice them in context.
tips: Tip: Confidence with fillers comes from positive exposure. Let the horse look, sniff, and process. Then ask it to jump. Patience wins.

title: Combination: In-and-Out at the Walk
steps: Set up two small fences 24 feet apart (one stride). This is an in-and-out combination.
Approach at the working walk. After the first fence, the horse has exactly one stride before the second.
Keep your leg on between the fences. The horse should jump in, stride, and jump out smoothly.
Your upper body should stay centered — don't lean forward between the fences.
Practice from both directions (the distances may feel different depending on the horse).
This is a standard combination found in almost every jumping course.
mistakes: The horse adding a stride (getting 2 strides instead of 1) due to lack of impulsion.
The rider getting ahead of the motion on the first fence and interfering with the stride.
Not enough leg between the fences, causing the horse to stop at the second element.
The horse jumping flat, not using its body correctly in the short distance.
variations: Change to a bounce (10 feet, no stride) for more technical demand.
Add a third element for a one-to-one-stride combination.
Raise the second element higher than the first for scope development.
tips: Tip: Combinations test your horse's training more than any other element. If the flatwork is correct, the combination rides itself.

title: Bounce to Bending Line at the Walk
steps: Set up a bounce (2 fences 10 feet apart), then a single fence on a bending line 60 feet from the bounce exit.
Walk into the bounce. The bounce develops quick reflexes and front leg technique.
After the bounce, walk on a bending line to the single fence in 4-5 strides.
The bending line requires you to establish a track and maintain rhythm immediately after the demanding bounce.
Practice the bending line to both left and right turns.
This combination tests both gymnastic ability and course-riding skill in one exercise.
mistakes: Losing balance in the bounce and not recovering in time for the bending line.
Not planning the bending line turn early enough — decide your direction before the bounce.
The canter after the bounce being too fast or unbalanced for the bending line.
Cutting the bending line too tight and arriving at the third fence too close.
variations: Change the bending line distance to 48 feet (3 strides direct, 4 strides bending).
Add a fourth fence after the bending line for a mini-course.
Replace the bounce with a one-stride gymnastic for a different entry.
tips: Tip: This exercise bridges the gap between gymnastic training and course riding. It teaches you to think quickly after a demanding effort.

title: Seeing the Distance: Single Fence Approach at the Walk
steps: Set up a single small cross-rail (12-18 inches). Approach at the working walk.
Focus on maintaining a steady rhythm — don't try to 'find' the distance by adjusting at the last moment.
Look at the top of the fence from 6 strides away. Keep your leg on and your rhythm steady.
If you arrive at a good distance, great. If it's slightly long or short, that's okay — the horse will adjust.
After each approach, evaluate: did the horse jump comfortably? Was the rhythm consistent?
Repeat 6-8 times. The goal is consistency of approach, not perfection of distance.
mistakes: Staring at the base of the fence, which drops the horse onto the forehand.
Making a major adjustment (pulling or kicking) in the last 2 strides.
Holding your breath on the approach, creating tension in your body.
Speed-up or slow-down approaches that change the rhythm in the last 3 strides.
variations: Approach from different angles: straight, slightly right, slightly left.
Close your eyes for 2 strides on the approach to develop feel.
Add a placing pole 9 feet before the fence to build distance confidence.
tips: Tip: The secret to seeing distances is not seeing them — it's maintaining rhythm. A steady canter produces good distances naturally.

title: Walk Pole Serpentine
steps: Set up 3 pairs of walk poles scattered across the arena, each pair spaced 4.5 feet apart.
Design a serpentine path that connects all three pairs with smooth curves.
Walk the serpentine, passing through each pair of poles on the bending line.
Maintain the walk rhythm through each pair and through each turn.
The turns between pole pairs develop the horse's ability to change bend while maintaining focus.
Repeat the serpentine 3-4 times, then reverse the direction for practice on the other rein.
mistakes: Cutting the turns too tight between pole pairs.
The horse speeding up between poles in anticipation.
Losing the trot rhythm over the poles because the approach angle isn't straight enough.
Not planning the turns far enough in advance.
variations: Raise the poles to cavaletti height for increased difficulty.
Add small cross-rails instead of ground poles.
Canter the serpentine with simple lead changes between pole pairs.
tips: Tip: Think of this as a mini-course without the jumps. The skills you develop here — turn planning, rhythm, line accuracy — directly transfer to course riding.

title: Vertical Jump: Building Height at the Walk
steps: Start with a vertical at 2 feet. Approach at the working walk.
Maintain rhythm and impulsion. The horse needs to use its hind legs to clear a vertical.
After 3 successful jumps at 2 feet, raise to 2'3". Repeat 3 clean jumps.
Continue raising in 3-inch increments: 2'6", 2'9", 3'0" (if your level permits).
At each height, evaluate: is the horse jumping cleanly? Is it rocking back on the haunches for takeoff?
Stop at the first sign of struggling or loss of form. Quality over height.
mistakes: Raising the fence before the horse is consistently clean at the current height.
Getting greedy with height and pushing past the horse's current ability.
The horse hanging its front legs because it's reaching instead of jumping with technique.
The rider getting behind the motion as the fences get bigger.
variations: Add a placing pole to maintain consistent takeoff distances.
Compare the horse's technique over a vertical vs. an oxer at the same height.
Use a gymnastic approach (trot in to one-stride) for height building.
tips: Tip: Height comes from technique, not courage. A horse that uses its body correctly at 2 feet will be safe at 3 feet.

The horse hanging its front legs because it's reaching instead of jumping with technique.
The rider getting behind the motion as the fences get bigger.
variations: Add a placing pole to maintain consistent takeoff distances.
Compare the horse's technique over a vertical vs. an oxer at the same height.
Use a gymnastic approach (trot in to one-stride) for height building.
tips: Tip: Height comes from technique, not courage. A horse that uses its body correctly at 2 feet will be safe at 3 feet.

title: Bounce Grid: Two Cross-Rails at the Walk
steps: Set up 2 small cross-rails 9-10 feet apart (bounce distance).
Approach at the walk. The horse will jump the first cross-rail, land, and immediately jump the second.
There is no stride between the fences — the horse bounces from one to the next.
Keep your upper body slightly behind vertical and maintain a steady leg contact.
Allow your arms to follow the horse's mouth over each jump. Don't drop the contact.
Repeat 4-6 times. Start with the cross-rails very low (12 inches) and gradually raise them.
mistakes: Approaching too fast, causing the horse to jump too big and land too far into the bounce.
The rider getting left behind on the first jump and interfering with the second.
Not enough impulsion, causing the horse to stop between the two fences.
Leaning too far forward, putting weight on the horse's forehand.
variations: Add a third cross-rail for a three-element bounce.
Raise the fences to verticals at 2 feet for more effort.
Place a ground pole one stride (18 feet) before the bounce as a placing pole.
tips: Tip: Bounces are the best gymnastic exercise. They teach the horse to use its body correctly without relying on speed.

steps: Set up 2 small cross-rails 9-10 feet apart (bounce distance).
Approach at the trot. The horse will jump the first cross-rail, land, and immediately jump the second.
There is no stride between the fences — the horse bounces from one to the next.
Keep your upper body slightly behind vertical and maintain a steady leg contact.
Allow your arms to follow the horse's mouth over each jump. Don't drop the contact.
Repeat 4-6 times. Start with the cross-rails very low (12 inches) and gradually raise them.
mistakes: Approaching too fast, causing the horse to jump too big and land too far into the bounce.
The rider getting left behind on the first jump and interfering with the second.
Not enough impulsion, causing the horse to stop between the two fences.
Leaning too far forward, putting weight on the horse's forehand.
variations: Add a third cross-rail for a three-element bounce.
Raise the fences to verticals at 2 feet for more effort.

tips: Tip: Bounces are the best gymnastic exercise. They teach the horse to use its body correctly without relying on speed.

There is no stride between the fences — the horse bounces from one to the next.

Repeat 4-6 times. Start with the cross-rails very low (12 inches) and gradually raise them.

variations: Add a third cross-rail for a three-element bounce.

Approach at the sitting trot. The horse will jump the first cross-rail, land, and immediately jump the second.

Approach at the collected trot. The horse will jump the first cross-rail, land, and immediately jump the second.
There is no stride between the fences — the horse bounces from one to the next.
Keep your upper body slightly behind vertical and maintain a steady leg contact.
Allow your arms to follow the horse's mouth over each jump. Don't drop the contact.
Repeat 4-6 times. Start with the cross-rails very low (12 inches) and gradually raise them.
mistakes: Approaching too fast, causing the horse to jump too big and land too far into the bounce.
